A perfume spray bottle uses it too — squeezing the bulb creates a high-speed air jet over a tube, lowering pressure, and atmospheric pressure pushes the perfume up!

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of water at 25°C | $\rho$ | **997** | kg/m³ |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma = \rho g$ | **9790** | N/m³ |
| Atmospheric pressure head | $P_{atm}/\rho g$ | **10.3** (approx) | m of water |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Bernoulli's Equation Step-by-Step

The Bernoulli's equation is derived from the **Euler's equation of motion** integrated along a streamline, combined with the **steady flow energy equation**. Here is the structured logic:

1. **Assumption of Steady Flow:** Fluid properties at any point do not change with time.
   $$\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0$$

2. **Incompressibility:** Fluid density $\rho$ is constant throughout the flow.
   $$\rho = \text{constant}$$

3. **Inviscid (Frictionless) Flow along a streamline:** No shear stresses between fluid layers.

4. **Conservation of Mass (Continuity):** For an incompressible fluid in a pipe of varying cross-section:
   $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q \text{ (constant discharge)}$$

5. **Newton's Second Law on a Fluid Element** along the streamline, integrated → **Bernoulli's Equation**.

6. **Real Fluid Correction:** In practice, friction causes a head loss $h_L$ between sections:
   $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU lab viva and exam question on this experiment.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric discharge / flow rate | m³/s |
| 2 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (turbulence check) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head (piezometric reading) | m |
| 4 | $\dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Velocity head (kinetic head) | m |
| 5 | $z$ | Datum head (elevation from reference) | m |
| 6 | $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g} + z$ | Total head at any section | m |
| 7 | $h_L = H_1 - H_2$ | Head loss between sections 1 and 2 | m |
| 8 | $v_i = \dfrac{Q}{A_i}$ | Velocity at section $i$ | m/s |
| 9 | $A_i = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_i^2$ | Cross-sectional area of pipe (circular) | m² |
| 10 | $\Delta H = H_{\text{theoretical}} - H_{\text{actual}}$ | Deviation / error in verification | m |
| 11 | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{H_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Percentage error in experiment | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical note on piezometric tubes:** In the Bernoulli's apparatus, the tubes are **piezometric (static) tubes**, NOT pitot tubes. They measure only the **static pressure head** $P/\rho g$. The **velocity head** is calculated separately from $v = Q/A$. The **total head** $H$ is then computed by summation — this is the core lab exercise.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Venturi meters** for flow measurement in pipelines.
- **Aerofoil design** — generation of lift in aircraft wings.
- **Carburetor and fuel injectors** — atomization of fuel using pressure drop.
- **Pitot-static tubes** in aircraft for measuring airspeed.
- **Turbine and pump draft tubes** — recovery of kinetic head to pressure head.
- **Notches and weirs** — open channel flow measurement.
- **Chimney design** — using stack effect for natural draft.

### 2.4 Why the Experiment Matters (The 'Why')

The theoretical Bernoulli's equation assumes **no friction**. In the lab, using real water flowing in a real pipe, friction **does** occur. The experiment:
1. Confirms that Bernoulli's equation is approximately valid in a converging-diverging section.
2. Quantifies the **head loss $h_L$** between sections.
3. Demonstrates the **continuity principle** (smaller area → higher velocity).
4. Teaches **energy accounting** — fluid mechanical energy is conserved, with some loss to friction (which becomes heat).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (for lab record)

#### Step 1: Consider a fluid element along a streamline

Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline. Let the element move from point **1** to point **2** under the action of:
- Pressure force on face 1: $P \, dA$
- Pressure force on face 2: $-(P + dP) \, dA$
- Weight component along flow direction: $- \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \cos\theta$

where $\theta$ is the angle the streamline makes with the horizontal.

#### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law

Mass of element: $dm = \rho \, dA \, ds$

Acceleration along streamline: $v \dfrac{dv}{ds}$

Net force along flow:

$$F_{net} = P \, dA - (P + dP) \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cos\theta$$

$$F_{net} = -dP \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cos\theta$$

#### Step 3: Newton's Second Law Equation

$$-dP \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cos\theta = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot v \frac{dv}{ds}$$

Divide through by $\rho \, dA \, ds$:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dP}{ds} - g\cos\theta = v \frac{dv}{ds}$$

#### Step 4: Geometric relationship

Since $dz = ds \cdot \cos\theta$, we have $\cos\theta = dz/ds$. Substituting:

$$-\frac{dP}{\rho} - g \, dz = v \, dv$$

#### Step 5: Integrate along the streamline from section 1 to section 2

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} -\frac{dP}{\rho} - \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} v \, dv$$

For incompressible flow ($\rho$ = constant):

$$-\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} - g(z_2 - z_1) = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2}$$

#### Step 6: Rearrange into Bernoulli's Equation

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

Dividing throughout by $g$ (to get energy per unit weight = head):

$$\boxed{\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2}$$

This is the **classical Bernoulli's equation** in head form. **Q.E.D.**

> [!NOTE]
> **In the KTU lab**, since the apparatus is horizontal (or the datum heads are equal), the $z$ terms cancel out, and the equation reduces to:
> $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Lab Calculation Procedure

The Bernoulli's experiment requires these systematic calculations at each section $i$:

| Step | Quantity | Formula | Source / Measurement |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diameter $d_i$ | Given | From apparatus spec sheet |
| 2 | Area $A_i$ | $A_i = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_i^2$ | Calculated |
| 3 | Discharge $Q$ | Collected volume / time | Stopwatch + measuring tank |
| 4 | Velocity $v_i$ | $v_i = Q / A_i$ | Calculated |
| 5 | Velocity head $vh_i$ | $vh_i = v_i^2 / (2g)$ | Calculated |
| 6 | Pressure head $ph_i$ | $ph_i = $ piezometer reading (h_i) | **Measured directly from piezometric tube** |
| 7 | Datum head $z_i$ | $z_i$ from apparatus | Reference line (often 0) |
| 8 | Total head $H_i$ | $H_i = ph_i + vh_i + z_i$ | Calculated |
| 9 | Head loss $h_{L,1-2}$ | $h_{L,1-2} = H_1 - H_2$ | Calculated |
| 10 | Theoretical $H_i$ (Bernoulli, no friction) | Use Bernoulli's equation with $h_L = 0$ | Reference value |
| 11 | % Error | $\dfrac{\vert H_{\text{theoretical}} - H_{\text{actual}} \vert}{H_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Calculated |

### 3.3 Typical Lab Observation Table Format (KTU Pattern)

| Section $i$ | Diameter $d_i$ (m) | Piezometer Reading $h_i$ (m) | Area $A_i$ (m²) | Velocity $v_i$ (m/s) | Velocity Head $v_i^2/2g$ (m) | Total Head $H_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_1$ | $h_1$ | $A_1$ | $v_1$ | $vh_1$ | $H_1$ |
| 2 | $d_2$ | $h_2$ | $A_2$ | $v_2$ | $vh_2$ | $H_2$ |
| 3 | $d_3$ | $h_3$ | $A_3$ | $v_3$ | $vh_3$ | $H_3$ |
| 4 | $d_4$ | $h_4$ | $A_4$ | $v_4$ | $vh_4$ | $H_4$ |
| 5 | $d_5$ | $h_5$ | $A_5$ | $v_5$ | $vh_5$ | $H_5$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Typical observation:** As we move from inlet (large diameter) toward the throat (smallest diameter), the **piezometer reading decreases** and the **velocity head increases**. The total head decreases very slightly (by $h_L$) due to friction.

### 3.4 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Pattern)

> **Problem:** Water flows through a horizontal Bernoulli's apparatus. At section 1, the diameter is 50 mm and piezometric head is 2.5 m. At section 2 (throat), the diameter is 25 mm and piezometric head is 1.8 m. The discharge measured is $Q = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s. Verify Bernoulli's theorem and find the head loss.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate velocities**

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1.963 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{1.963 \times 10^{-3}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate velocity heads**

$$vh_1 = \frac{(1.0)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.0510 \text{ m}$$

$$vh_2 = \frac{(4.0)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.8155 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Total head at each section** (horizontal, so $z_1 = z_2 = 0$)

$$H_1 = ph_1 + vh_1 = 2.5 + 0.0510 = 2.5510 \text{ m}$$

$$H_2 = ph_2 + vh_2 = 1.8 + 0.8155 = 2.6155 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Head loss**

$$h_L = H_1 - H_2 = 2.5510 - 2.6155 = -0.0645 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The negative sign here indicates a sign convention issue. In reality, head loss is always $h_L \geq 0$. The student must check which section is **upstream** (inlet). Here Section 1 is upstream, so the slight discrepancy is due to **measurement error** (typically $\leq 5\%$). If $H_2 > H_1$ slightly, it implies the second section was actually upstream or measurement error. The correct sign convention is: $h_L = H_{\text{upstream}} - H_{\text{downstream}} \geq 0$.

**Step 6: Percentage error** (using mean total head as reference)

$$H_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2.5510 + 2.6155}{2} = 2.5833 \text{ m}$$

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert H_1 - H_2 \vert}{H_{\text{mean}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0645}{2.5833} \times 100 \approx 2.5\%$$

Since this is within the acceptable lab tolerance of **5%**, Bernoulli's theorem is **verified**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bernoulli's Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional interactions of the laboratory setup used to verify Bernoulli's theorem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Overhead Tank\nConstant Head Supply] --> B[Sump Tank\nWater Reservoir]
    B --> C[Centrifugal Pump\nProvides Flow]
    C --> D[Control Valve\nRegulates Discharge]
    D --> E[Bernoulli Apparatus\nConverging Diverging Test Section]
    E --> F[Piezometric Tubes\nStatic Pressure Taps]
    E --> G[Outlet to Measuring Tank]
    G --> H[Volumetric Tank\nCollected Volume]
    H --> I[Stopwatch\nTime Measurement]
    F --> J[Observation Table\nPressure Head Readings]
    I --> K[Calculation Block\nQ, v, Velocity Head]
    J --> L[Total Head Computation\nH equals P by rho g plus v square by 2g plus z]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Verification of Bernoulli Theorem\nPlot H versus Section Position]
    M --> N[Result: Total Head approximately constant\nHead Loss determined]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Schematic — Converging-Diverging Test Section

The test section typically has **5 to 7 pressure tappings** along its length, with diameter varying from inlet (large) to throat (small) to outlet (medium).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Section 1\nInlet\nLargest Diameter d1\nPiezometer Tube 1] --> P2[Section 2\nConverging\nDiameter d2\nPiezometer Tube 2]
    P2 --> P3[Section 3\nThroat\nSmallest Diameter d3\nPiezometer Tube 3]
    P3 --> P4[Section 4\nDiverging\nDiameter d4\nPiezometer Tube 4]
    P4 --> P5[Section 5\nOutlet\nDiameter d5\nPiezometer Tube 5]
    P1 -.Pressure tap line 1.-> T1[Piezometer Manometer Bank]
    P2 -.Pressure tap line 2.-> T1
    P3 -.Pressure tap line 3.-> T1
    P4 -.Pressure tap line 4.-> T1
    P5 -.Pressure tap line 5.-> T1
    T1 --> Obs[Direct Visual Reading\nPiezometric Heads h1 to h5]
```

### 4.3 Energy Grade Line vs Hydraulic Grade Line (Conceptual Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FL[Flow Through Converging Diverging Pipe]
        direction LR
        S1[Section 1\nHGL high\nEGL higher] --> S2[Section 2\nHGL drops\nEGL drops slightly]
        S2 --> S3[Section 3 Throat\nHGL lowest\nEGL lowest at throat]
        S3 --> S4[Section 4\nHGL rises\nEGL rises slightly]
        S4 --> S5[Section 5\nHGL recovers partially\nEGL has small head loss]
    end
    S1 -.Hydraulic Grade Line.-> S5
    S1 -.Energy Grade Line.-> S5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **HGL** (Hydraulic Grade Line) = $P/\rho g + z$ — pressure head + datum head
> **EGL** (Energy Grade Line) = $P/\rho g + v^2/2g + z$ — HGL + velocity head
> The vertical gap between EGL and HGL at any section equals the **velocity head** $v^2/2g$.
> The slope of the EGL represents the **frictional head loss per unit length**.

### 4.4 Observation-to-Result Workflow (Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Experiment]) --> St1[Step 1: Note diameters of all sections from apparatus]
    St1 --> St2[Step 2: Open control valve to set desired flow]
    St2 --> St3[Step 3: Wait for steady state in piezometer tubes]
    St3 --> St4[Step 4: Record piezometer readings h1 h2 h3 h4 h5]
    St4 --> St5[Step 5: Collect water in measuring tank for time t]
    St5 --> St6[Step 6: Calculate Q = Volume / Time]
    St6 --> St7[Step 7: For each section calculate v = Q / A]
    St7 --> St8[Step 8: Calculate velocity head v square by 2g]
    St8 --> St9[Step 9: Calculate total head H at each section]
    St9 --> St10[Step 10: Tabulate all values and plot H versus section]
    St10 --> St11[Step 11: Compare with theoretical Bernoulli prediction]
    St11 --> Decision{Is H approximately constant?}
    Decision -- Yes --> Pass[Bernoulli Theorem Verified\nHead Loss equals H1 minus H2]
    Decision -- No --> Fail[Check for errors\nAir bubbles, leaks, calibration]
    Pass --> End([End Experiment])
    Fail --> St2
```

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State Bernoulli's theorem. Write its mathematical form in terms of head.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1.5 marks):** For a steady, incompressible, irrotational flow of an ideal fluid (inviscid) along a streamline, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and datum (elevation) head is constant.
>
> **Mathematical form (1.5 marks):**
> $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \text{Constant}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What are the assumptions made in the derivation of Bernoulli's equation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The assumptions are:
1. **Steady flow** — fluid properties do not change with time. (0.5 mark)
2. **Incompressible flow** — density $\rho$ is constant. (0.5 mark)
3. **Inviscid (frictionless) flow** — no viscous shear stresses. (0.5 mark)
4. **Flow along a streamline** — equation is valid along a single streamline (or throughout the flow if irrotational). (0.5 mark)
5. **No energy addition or extraction** — no pump, turbine, or heat transfer between sections. (0.5 mark)
6. **Newtonian fluid** — follows Newton's law of viscosity. (0.5 mark)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert **diameter from mm to m** before calculating area. This causes velocities off by a factor of 1000!
> - **Common mistake #2:** Using **$g = 10$ m/s²** instead of the standard **9.81 m/s²**. KTU examiners check this.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Not maintaining **sign convention** in $h_L = H_1 - H_2$. Always use **upstream – downstream** so $h_L \geq 0$.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Recording piezometer readings in cm and velocity heads in m **without unit conversion** — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - **Common mistake #5:** Forgetting to plot the **H vs section** graph — this carries **2 marks** in the lab report assessment.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) Derive Bernoulli's equation from Euler's equation of motion. State clearly the assumptions involved. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. Steady flow
2. Incompressible flow
3. Inviscid (frictionless) flow
4. Flow along a streamline
5. No external work / no pump or turbine between sections

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Consider a fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline between points 1 and 2.

Forces on the element along the streamline:
- Pressure force on face 1: $+P \, dA$
- Pressure force on face 2: $-(P + dP) \, dA$
- Weight component along flow: $-\rho g \, dA \, ds \cos\theta$

Applying Newton's second law (mass × acceleration):

$$-dP \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cos\theta = (\rho \, dA \, ds) \cdot v \frac{dv}{ds}$$

Dividing by $\rho \, dA \, ds$ and using $\cos\theta = dz/ds$:

$$-\frac{dP}{\rho} - g \, dz = v \, dv$$

Integrating from section 1 to 2 (with $\rho$ = constant):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

This is Bernoulli's equation. **Q.E.D.**

---

**(b) In a horizontal Bernoulli's apparatus, the diameter at inlet section is 60 mm and at the throat is 30 mm. The piezometric head at inlet is 3.2 m and at throat is 2.5 m. The discharge is $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s. Find the head loss between the sections and verify Bernoulli's theorem. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d_1 = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m, $d_2 = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- $h_1 = 3.2$ m, $h_2 = 2.5$ m
- $Q = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s², horizontal apparatus ($z_1 = z_2 = 0$)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.060)^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Velocities**

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.8843 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.537 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Velocity heads**

$$vh_1 = \frac{(0.8843)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.03986 \text{ m}$$

$$vh_2 = \frac{(3.537)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.6376 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Total heads**

$$H_1 = 3.2 + 0.03986 + 0 = 3.2399 \text{ m}$$

$$H_2 = 2.5 + 0.6376 + 0 = 3.1376 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Head loss**

$$h_L = H_1 - H_2 = 3.2399 - 3.1376 = 0.1023 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Percentage error**

$$H_{\text{mean}} = \frac{3.2399 + 3.1376}{2} = 3.1888 \text{ m}$$

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{0.1023}{3.1888} \times 100 = 3.21\%$$

**Step 7 (1 mark): Conclusion**

Since the percentage error is **less than 5%** (the acceptable lab tolerance), **Bernoulli's theorem is verified**. The head loss between the inlet and throat is **0.1023 m**, which is due to frictional effects in real fluid flow.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternate]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup used to verify Bernoulli's theorem. List the observations and the formula used for head loss calculation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Description of Setup (3 marks):**

The Bernoulli's apparatus consists of:
1. **Sump tank** – stores water at the bottom of the setup.
2. **Centrifugal pump** – pumps water from the sump to the overhead tank.
3. **Overhead tank (constant head tank)** – maintains a constant water level for steady flow.
4. **Control valve** – regulates the discharge through the test section.
5. **Converging-diverging test section** – a transparent pipe that converges to a throat and then diverges, fitted with **5–7 pressure tappings** (piezometric tubes) along its length.
6. **Piezometric tubes** – vertical transparent tubes connected to the pressure tappings, indicating the **static pressure head** at each section.
7. **Measuring tank with stopwatch** – for measuring the volumetric discharge.
8. **Outlet** – discharges the flow into the measuring tank.

**Working:** Water flows from the overhead tank through the test section. The piezometric tubes show the pressure head at each section. A known volume of water is collected over a measured time to compute discharge $Q$. Velocities are then computed from continuity ($v = Q/A$), and total heads are tabulated to verify Bernoulli's theorem.

**Observations Recorded (2 marks):**
- Diameter of pipe at each section ($d_i$)
- Piezometer readings ($h_i$) at each section
- Volume of water collected ($V$)
- Time taken ($t$)

**Formula for Head Loss (2 marks):**

$$h_L = H_1 - H_2 = \left(\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1\right) - \left(\frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2\right)$$

For horizontal apparatus: $z_1 = z_2$, so

$$h_L = (h_1 - h_2) + \frac{v_1^2 - v_2^2}{2g}$$

where $h_i$ is the piezometric reading and $v_i$ is the velocity at section $i$.

---

**(b) The following observations were recorded in a Bernoulli's experiment. The test section is horizontal. The discharge is $3.14 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s. Verify Bernoulli's theorem and calculate the head loss between sections 1 and 3. (7 marks)**

| Section | Diameter (mm) | Piezometer Head (m) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 2.80 |
| 2 | 30 | 2.20 |
| 3 | 40 | 2.65 |

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $Q = 3.14 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 (1.5 marks): Calculate areas and velocities**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \Rightarrow v_1 = \frac{3.14 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.6 \text{ m/s}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \Rightarrow v_2 = \frac{3.14 \times 10^{-3}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.443 \text{ m/s}$$

$$A_3 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.040)^2 = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \Rightarrow v_3 = \frac{3.14 \times 10^{-3}}{1.257 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.498 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Calculate velocity heads**

$$vh_1 = \frac{(1.6)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.1305 \text{ m}$$

$$vh_2 = \frac{(4.443)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 1.006 \text{ m}$$

$$vh_3 = \frac{(2.498)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.3180 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Calculate total heads at sections 1 and 3**

$$H_1 = 2.80 + 0.1305 + 0 = 2.9305 \text{ m}$$

$$H_3 = 2.65 + 0.3180 + 0 = 2.9680 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Head loss between sections 1 and 3**

$$h_{L, 1-3} = H_1 - H_3 = 2.9305 - 2.9680 = -0.0375 \text{ m}$$

The negative value indicates that, in this case, $H_3 > H_1$, which is physically not possible for actual flow (friction should cause a loss). This shows that the data has measurement inconsistencies, or section 3 is **upstream** of section 1 in the actual flow path (the converging-diverging arrangement typically has section 1 → section 2 (throat) → section 3, but the diameters here suggest section 2 is the throat, and section 3 is downstream of it). The magnitude of deviation:

$$\vert h_{L, 1-3} \vert = 0.0375 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks): Verification and conclusion**

$$H_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2.9305 + 2.9680}{2} = 2.949 \text{ m}$$

$$\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{0.0375}{2.949} \times 100 = 1.27\%$$

Since the deviation is **less than 5%**, the experimental data **approximately verifies Bernoulli's theorem**. The small discrepancy is attributed to:
- Measurement errors in piezometer readings
- Air bubbles trapped in piezometric tubes
- Friction losses in the pipe
- Calibration errors of the measuring tank

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision for KTU Lab Exam / Viva — Memorize these points!**

- [x] **Bernoulli's Theorem** = Conservation of mechanical energy along a streamline for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.
- [x] **Three heads:** Pressure head ($P/\rho g$), Velocity head ($v^2/2g$), Datum head ($z$).
- [x] **Sum of three heads** = Total head $H$ = **constant** (ideal case) or decreases by $h_L$ (real case).
- [x] **Continuity equation:** $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ → smaller area → higher velocity → lower pressure.
- [x] **In the lab apparatus:** Throat (smallest diameter) has **lowest piezometer reading** and **highest velocity head**.
- [x] **Discharge formula:** $Q = V / t$ (volume collected / time taken).
- [x] **Velocity at section:** $v_i = Q / A_i$ where $A_i = (\pi/4) d_i^2$.
- [x] **Head loss:** $h_L = H_{\text{upstream}} - H_{\text{downstream}}$, always $\geq 0$.
- [x] **Acceptance criterion:** Percentage error $\leq 5\%$ → Bernoulli's theorem **verified**.
- [x] **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** — NOT 10.
- [x] **Convert all diameters to meters** before calculating area.
- [x] **Assumptions of Bernoulli:** Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along streamline, no pump/turbine.
- [x] **Piezometric tubes measure static pressure only** — NOT stagnation pressure.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Venturi meter, aerofoils, carburetor, pitot tube, draft tube, Bunsen burner.
- [x] **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL):** Plots $P/\rho g + z$ vs. section.
- [x] **Energy Grade Line (EGL):** Plots $P/\rho g + v^2/2g + z$ vs. section. Lies above HGL by $v^2/2g$.
- [x] **Common viva questions:**
  - *Why does pressure drop at the throat?* → Velocity increases, energy redistribution.
  - *Why is the total head not exactly constant?* → Real fluid has friction (viscous losses).
  - *What is the difference between static, dynamic, and stagnation pressure?* → Static = $P$, Dynamic = $\rho v^2/2$, Stagnation = $P + \rho v^2/2$.
  - *Why are piezometer tubes used and not pitot tubes?* → Piezometers measure static pressure, which is what Bernoulli's equation requires.
  - *What is the role of the constant head tank?* → Maintains steady flow by ensuring constant upstream pressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Metacentric Height and Radius of Gyration of Floating Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Metacentric Height and Radius of Gyration of Floating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Metacentric Height (GM)** is the distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of a floating body and the **Metacenter (M)** of the body. It is a measure of the *static stability* of a floating body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacenter (M)** is defined as the point of intersection of the **vertical line passing through the new Center of Buoyancy (B′)** with the **original vertical line passing through the Center of Buoyancy (B)** in the equilibrium position, when the body is given a small angular displacement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Radius of Gyration (K)** of a floating body is the distance from the axis of rotation at which the entire area of the waterline plane can be assumed to be concentrated, such that its second moment of area equals the actual second moment of the waterline plane. Mathematically, $K = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$, where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane and $A$ is the area of the waterline plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Center of Buoyancy (B)** is the centroid of the **submerged volume** of fluid displaced by the floating body, and through this point, the **buoyant force** acts vertically upward.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a **small wooden boat** floating on a calm lake. The boat has a flat bottom, and the water exerts an upward push (buoyancy) on it. Your weight acts downward through your **Center of Gravity (G)**, while the buoyancy acts upward through the **Center of Buoyancy (B)** — the geometric center of the underwater portion of the boat.

Now, slowly shift your weight to one side of the boat. The boat **tilts (lists)** slightly. The waterline shifts, and the underwater shape changes, causing the **center of buoyancy to migrate toward the side you moved to**. A new vertical line through this new buoyancy center intersects the original vertical (drawn through the old buoyancy center) at a point called the **Metacenter (M)**.

- If **M lies above G** → the body is **stable** (it returns to upright).
- If **M lies below G** → the body is **unstable** (it capsizes).
- If **M coincides with G** → the body is in **neutral equilibrium**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the **Metacenter (M)** as the "balance pivot." The higher it sits above the Center of Gravity, the more "top-heavy" stability the floating body has — like a tightrope walker with a long pole.

The **Radius of Gyration (K)** is a geometric property of the waterline cross-section. It tells us how "wide" the floating body is at the waterline. The wider the body, the larger the K, and therefore the larger the BM (and hence the metacentric height) for a given submerged volume.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of fresh water | $\rho_w$ | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Density of sea water | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | **9810** | N/m³ |
| Small-angle assumption | $\theta$ | $< 10°$ | degrees |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Concept of Stability in Floating Bodies

A floating body is in **stable equilibrium** if, when slightly displaced from its equilibrium position, it develops a **restoring couple** that brings it back to the original position. The metacentric height is the direct measure of this stability.

**Three Conditions of Equilibrium:**

| Condition | Metacentric Height | Stability State |
|---|---|---|
| $GM > 0$ (M above G) | Positive | **Stable** (restoring couple exists) |
| $GM = 0$ (M at G) | Zero | **Neutral** (no restoring couple) |
| $GM < 0$ (M below G) | Negative | **Unstable** (capsizing couple exists) |

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework: Why Does the Body Restore?

When a floating body is tilted by a small angle $\theta$:
1. The **Center of Gravity (G)** remains in its original position (since the body is rigid and mass distribution is unchanged).
2. The **Center of Buoyancy (B)** shifts to a new position **B′** because the submerged volume geometry changes.
3. The new vertical line through **B′** intersects the original line of action of buoyancy at **M** (the Metacenter).
4. The buoyant force $W$ acting upward through **B′** and the weight $W$ acting downward through **G** form a **couple** of magnitude $W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$.
5. For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$, so the restoring couple $\approx W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In naval architecture, a positive GM is critical. A very high GM, however, makes the ship **stiff** (rapid rolling, uncomfortable). A very low GM makes the ship **tender** (slow rolling, but risk of capsizing). Naval designers aim for an *optimal GM* — typically **0.15 m to 0.50 m** for ocean-going vessels.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$ | Buoyancy equals weight at equilibrium | $W$: weight, $V_{sub}$: submerged volume |
| 2 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}}$ | Distance B to M | $I$: 2nd moment of waterline area |
| 3 | $BM = \dfrac{K^2 \cdot A}{V_{sub}}$ | BM in terms of radius of gyration | $K$: radius of gyration, $A$: waterline area |
| 4 | $GM = BM - BG$ | Metacentric height (G above B) | $BG$: distance between B and G |
| 5 | $GM = BM + BG$ | Metacentric height (G below B) | Used for solid floating bodies |
| 6 | $K = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Radius of gyration of waterline area | $I$: 2nd moment, $A$: waterline area |
| 7 | $GM_{exp} = \dfrac{P \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ | **Experimental GM (KTU Lab formula)** | $P$: shifted weight, $x$: distance moved |
| 8 | $K_{exp} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(GM+BG) \cdot V_{sub}}{A}}$ | **Experimental radius of gyration** | Derived from BM relation |
| 9 | $K_{ideal} = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | Theoretical K for rectangular waterline | $B$: breadth of pontoon |
| 10 | Restoring Couple $= W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Restoring moment on tilt | For finite angle $\theta$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — Sign Convention for BG:**
> - If **G lies above B** (typical for hollow vessels/pontoons) → use $GM = BM - BG$
> - If **G lies below B** (typical for solid bodies) → use $GM = BM + BG$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Naval Architecture** | Ship design, submarine stability, pontoon bridges |
| **Civil Engineering** | Floating docks, ferry terminals, buoyancy platforms |
| **Offshore Engineering** | Oil rigs, semi-submersible platforms, FPSOs |
| **Hydraulic Engineering** | Dredgers, floating cranes, barges |
| **Aerospace** | Buoyancy-assisted vehicles, amphibious aircraft floats |

The metacentric height determination is a **mandatory sea trial** for every new ship class before it is certified for passenger or cargo service.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Metacentric Height Formula ($GM = BM - BG$)

**Step 1: Consider a body floating in equilibrium.**
A body of weight $W$ floats in a liquid of specific weight $\rho g$. The center of gravity is at **G** and the center of buoyancy is at **B**, both lying on the original vertical axis of symmetry.

**Step 2: Apply a small angular tilt $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis.**
The body is rotated by a small angle $\theta$. The center of gravity **G remains at the same point** (since the body is rigid). The center of buoyancy **shifts from B to B′** because the volume of displaced water changes shape (more water displaced on the submerged side, less on the emerged side).

**Step 3: Locate the Metacenter M.**
A vertical line is drawn through the new center of buoyancy **B′**. This line intersects the original vertical line (through old **B**) at a point **M**, which is the **Metacenter**.

**Step 4: Construct the geometry.**
The original buoyancy force $W$ acts vertically upward through **B**. The new buoyancy force $W$ acts vertically upward through **B′**. The weight $W$ acts downward through **G**.

The horizontal distance between the lines of action of $W$ (upward through B′) and $W$ (downward through G) is $\overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$.

**Step 5: Apply equilibrium of moments.**
The couple formed by the two equal and opposite forces $W$ is:

$$
\text{Restoring Couple} = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta
$$

For **small angles** (KTU standard: $\theta < 10°$), $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians). Therefore:

$$
\text{Restoring Couple} = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \tan\theta
$$

**Step 6: Establish the BM relation.**
From the geometry of the displaced water volume, it can be shown (by integrating the moment of elementary buoyant forces) that the shift in the center of buoyancy produces:

$$
\overline{BM} = \frac{I}{V_{sub}}
$$

where $I$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterline plane about the longitudinal axis of tilting, and $V_{sub}$ is the submerged volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof of BM = I/V_sub:** When the body tilts by $\theta$, an elementary area $dA$ of the waterline moves a vertical distance $x\tan\theta$ (where $x$ is the horizontal distance from the tilting axis). The buoyant force change over this area is $\rho g \cdot dA \cdot x \tan\theta$. Taking moments about the tilting axis and integrating over the waterline area:
> $$\int \rho g \cdot dA \cdot x \tan\theta \cdot x = \rho g \tan\theta \int x^2 dA = \rho g \tan\theta \cdot I$$
> Setting this equal to the restoring couple $W \cdot \overline{BM} \cdot \tan\theta$ gives $\overline{BM} = I / V_{sub}$.

**Step 7: Combine to get the metacentric height.**
From the geometry of the triangle $GBM$:

$$
GM = BM - BG \quad \text{(when G is above B)}
$$

Therefore, the **complete metacentric height equation** is:

$$
\boxed{GM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG = \frac{K^2 A}{V_{sub}} - BG}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Experimental GM Formula ($GM = \frac{Px}{W \tan\theta}$)

**Step 1: Setup the experiment.**
A floating pontoon carries a small "jockey weight" $P$ that can be moved horizontally across the deck by a known distance $x$.

**Step 2: Apply the weight shift.**
The jockey weight $P$ is moved by distance $x$ from the central axis. This creates an **overturning moment**:

$$
\text{Overturning Moment} = P \cdot x
$$

**Step 3: The pontoon tilts by angle $\theta$.**
The body rotates until the **overturning moment is balanced by the restoring moment**:

$$
P \cdot x = W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta
$$

**Step 4: Solve for GM.**

$$
\boxed{GM = \frac{P \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta}}
$$

This is the **primary KTU lab formula** for the experiment.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Radius of Gyration ($K$)

From the formula $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}}$ and the definition $I = K^2 \cdot A$:

$$
BM = \frac{K^2 \cdot A}{V_{sub}}
$$

Rearranging for $K$:

$$
K^2 = \frac{BM \cdot V_{sub}}{A}
$$

Since $BM = GM + BG$ (when G is above B):

$$
\boxed{K = \sqrt{\frac{(GM + BG) \cdot V_{sub}}{A_{waterline}}}
$$

For a **rectangular pontoon** of length $L$ and breadth $B$ (tilting about the longitudinal axis along $L$):

$$
I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}, \quad A = L \cdot B
$$

$$
K_{ideal} = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{B^2}{12}} = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{B\sqrt{3}}{6}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
A rectangular pontoon of length **1.5 m**, breadth **1.0 m**, and height **0.5 m** weighs **500 N**. It floats in water with a depth of immersion of **0.1 m**. A jockey weight of **30 N** is moved across the deck by **0.4 m**, causing the pontoon to tilt by **4°**. Determine:
(a) The experimental metacentric height.
(b) The experimental radius of gyration of the waterline area.

**Given Data:**
- $L = 1.5$ m, $B = 1.0$ m, $H = 0.5$ m
- $W = 500$ N, $d = 0.1$ m (depth of immersion)
- $P = 30$ N, $x = 0.4$ m, $\theta = 4°$

**Solution (a): Metacentric Height**

$$
\tan 4° = 0.0699
$$

$$
GM = \frac{P \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta} = \frac{30 \times 0.4}{500 \times 0.0699}
$$

$$
GM = \frac{12.0}{34.95} = 0.3434 \text{ m}
$$

**Solution (b): Radius of Gyration**

Submerged volume:

$$
V_{sub} = L \cdot B \cdot d = 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.1 = 0.15 \text{ m}^3
$$

Waterline area:

$$
A = L \cdot B = 1.5 \times 1.0 = 1.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

Position of B (from the bottom):

$$
\overline{B} = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{0.1}{2} = 0.05 \text{ m}
$$

Position of G (geometric center, from the bottom):

$$
\overline{G} = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m}
$$

Distance BG (G above B):

$$
BG = \overline{G} - \overline{B} = 0.25 - 0.05 = 0.20 \text{ m}
$$

Since G is above B: $BM = GM + BG$

$$
BM = 0.3434 + 0.20 = 0.5434 \text{ m}
$$

Now, applying the K relation:

$$
K^2 = \frac{BM \cdot V_{sub}}{A} = \frac{0.5434 \times 0.15}{1.5} = 0.05434 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{K = \sqrt{0.05434} = 0.2331 \text{ m}}
$$

**Verification (Theoretical K for rectangular section):**

$$
K_{ideal} = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1.0}{2 \times 1.732} = 0.2887 \text{ m}
$$

**Comparison:** $K_{exp} = 0.2331$ m $< K_{ideal} = 0.2887$ m — this discrepancy is expected due to frictional losses, small-angle approximations, and measurement errors. The **percentage error** is:

$$
\% \text{Error} = \frac{0.2887 - 0.2331}{0.2887} \times 100 = 19.26\%
$$

A KTU-acceptable result is typically within **15%–20%** of the theoretical value.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sources of Error in the Experiment

| Source of Error | Effect on GM | Effect on K | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Friction at pivot points | Reduces measured $\theta$ | Overestimates GM, underestimates K | Use low-friction bearings |
| Parallax in tilt scale | Inaccurate $\theta$ | Both affected | Use mirror-backed scale |
| Water surface oscillations | Fluctuating $\theta$ | Erratic readings | Allow water to settle |
| Inaccurate jockey weight placement | Wrong $x$ | Both affected | Use Vernier scale |
| Body not centrally loaded initially | Pre-tilt | Biased GM | Center pontoon first |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Experimental Setup Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete **KTU 2024 Standard Laboratory Setup** for the determination of metacentric height and radius of gyration of a floating body.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TankAssembly [WATER TANK ASSEMBLY]
        A1[Water Tank - Rectangular Glass Tank]
        A2[Static Water Surface]
    end

    subgraph FloatingBody [FLOATING PONTOON]
        B1[Rectangular Hollow Pontoon]
        B2[Deck of Pontoon]
        B3[Hull Below Waterline]
        B4[Pivot / Trunnion Bearing]
    end

    subgraph LoadingSystem [WEIGHT SHIFTING SYSTEM]
        C1[Jockey Weight - P]
        C2[Position Scale - x in m]
        C3[Sliding Rail on Deck]
    end

    subgraph MeasurementSystem [TILT MEASUREMENT SYSTEM]
        D1[Plumb Bob]
        D2[Tilt Scale - Angle theta]
        D3[Pointer Arm]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B3
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> C3
    C3 --> C1
    C3 --> C2
    B4 --> D3
    D3 --> D1
    D3 --> D2

    F1[Weight W of Pontoon - measured on Weighing Balance] --> B1
    F2[Dimensions L B H of Pontoon] --> B1
    F3[Depth of Immersion d - measured] --> B3

    classDef tankStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    classDef bodyStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    classDef loadStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    classDef measureStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    class A1,A2 tankStyle
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 bodyStyle
    class C1,C2,C3 loadStyle
    class D1,D2,D3 measureStyle
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Force and Geometry Diagram (Tilted Body)

The following diagram represents the **geometric relationship** between the Center of Gravity (G), Center of Buoyancy (B), Metacenter (M), and the resulting couple when the body tilts by angle $\theta$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph EquilibriumState [EQUILIBRIUM - UNTI LTED]
        E1[Original Center of Buoyancy B]
        E2[Center of Gravity G - same position]
        E3[Vertical Line through B and G]
    end

    subgraph TiltedState [TILTED STATE - angle theta]
        T1[New Center of Buoyancy B prime]
        T2[Vertical Line through B prime]
        T3[Weight W acting downward through G]
        T4[Buoyant Force W acting upward through B prime]
    end

    subgraph KeyPoints [KEY METACENTRIC POINTS]
        K1[Metacenter M - intersection point]
        K2[Distance BM = I / Vsub]
        K3[Distance BG - vertical gap]
        K4[Distance GM = BM - BG]
    end

    subgraph Restoring [RESTORING MECHANISM]
        R1[Restoring Couple = W times GM times tan theta]
        R2[Stability: GM greater than 0 - Stable]
    end

    E3 --> T2
    E1 -.shifts to.-> T1
    T2 --> K1
    E3 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    E1 --> K3
    E2 --> K3
    K1 --> K4
    T1 --> T4
    E2 --> T3
    T3 --> R1
    T4 --> R1
    R1 --> R2

    classDef equilStyle fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#007bff
    classDef tiltStyle fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#ff4444
    classDef keyStyle fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#ffaa00
    classDef restStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    class E1,E2,E3 equilStyle
    class T1,T2,T3,T4 tiltStyle
    class K1,K2,K3,K4 keyStyle
    class R1,R2 restStyle
```

### 4.3 Data Recording Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph InputStage [MEASURED INPUTS]
        I1[Weight of Pontoon W in N]
        I2[Dimensions L B H in m]
        I3[Jockey Weight P in N]
        I4[Distance Moved x in m]
        I5[Angle of Tilt theta in degrees]
    end

    subgraph ComputationStage [CALCULATION STEPS]
        C1[Compute tan theta]
        C2[Compute GM = Px divided by W tan theta]
        C3[Compute Vsub and A waterline]
        C4[Compute BG from geometry]
        C5[Compute BM = GM plus BG]
        C6[Compute K = sqrt BM times Vsub divided by A]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage [RESULTS]
        O1[Experimental GM in m]
        O2[Experimental K in m]
        O3[Theoretical K ideal = B by 2 sqrt 3]
        O4[Percentage Error]
        O5[Stability Verdict]
    end

    I1 --> C2
    I3 --> C2
    I4 --> C2
    I5 --> C1
    I1 --> C3
    I2 --> C3
    I2 --> C4
    I1 --> C3
    I3 --> C3
    C2 --> C5
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C6
    C3 --> C6
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> O1
    C6 --> O2
    I2 --> O3
    O2 --> O4
    O3 --> O4
    O1 --> O5

    classDef inputStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    classDef calcStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ff8800
    classDef outputStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    class I1,I2,I3,I4,I5 inputStyle
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6 calcStyle
    class O1,O2,O3,O4,O5 outputStyle
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define metacentric height. State the conditions of stability for a floating body.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** The metacentric height of a floating body is defined as the distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of the body and the **Metacenter (M)**. It is denoted by **GM**.

> **Conditions of Stability (2 Marks):**
> - **Stable Equilibrium:** When **GM > 0** (Metacenter M lies *above* the Center of Gravity G). The body, when tilted, experiences a restoring couple and returns to its original position.
> - **Neutral Equilibrium:** When **GM = 0** (M coincides with G). The body remains in the tilted position with no restoring couple.
> - **Unstable Equilibrium:** When **GM < 0** (M lies *below* G). The body capsizes, as the couple is overturning rather than restoring.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is the difference between the Center of Gravity and the Center of Buoyancy of a floating body?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Center of Gravity (G) | Center of Buoyancy (B) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | It is the point through which the **weight** of the body is considered to act vertically **downward**. | It is the point through which the **buoyant force** (upthrust) is considered to act vertically **upward**. |
| 2 | It is the centroid of the **entire body mass** (above and below water). | It is the centroid of the **submerged volume** of fluid displaced. |
| 3 | Its position **does not change** when the body is tilted (for a rigid body). | Its position **shifts** to a new location (B′) when the body is tilted. |
| 4 | Determined by the **mass distribution** of the body. | Determined by the **geometry** of the submerged portion. |

*(Full 3 marks awarded for correct tabular differentiation with at least 3 valid points.)*

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Regular]**
**Define the radius of gyration of a floating body. How is it related to the metacentric height?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The radius of gyration ($K$) of the waterline area of a floating body is defined as the distance from the axis of rotation at which the entire waterline area can be assumed to be concentrated such that its moment of inertia equals the actual second moment of area of the waterline plane.
> 
> Mathematically: $K = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$, where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane and $A$ is the waterline area.

> **Relation to Metacentric Height (1.5 Marks):** The radius of gyration is related to the metacentric height through the equation:
> 
> $$GM = \frac{K^2 \cdot A}{V_{sub}} - BG$$
> 
> This shows that for a given submerged volume and BG, a larger K (i.e., a wider waterline) produces a larger GM, and hence greater stability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q1A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Regular]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the metacentric height of a floating body. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular pontoon of length 2 m, breadth 1.2 m, and height 0.6 m weighs 800 N. It floats in water with a depth of immersion of 0.12 m. A weight of 40 N is moved across the pontoon by 0.5 m, causing it to tilt by 5°. Calculate the metacentric height and the radius of gyration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a): Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Consider a body of weight $W$ floating in equilibrium. Its center of gravity is at $G$ and center of buoyancy at $B$, both on the same vertical line.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** The body is given a small angular tilt $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis. The center of buoyancy shifts to $B'$. The new vertical through $B'$ meets the original vertical (through $B$) at the metacenter $M$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** The buoyant force $W$ acting upward through $B'$ and the weight $W$ acting downward through $G$ form a couple. The moment arm is $\overline{GM} \sin\theta$.

Restoring couple:

$$
\text{Restoring Couple} = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** The distance $BM$ is obtained by considering the geometry of the displaced water:

$$
BM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} = \frac{K^2 \cdot A}{V_{sub}}
$$

From triangle $GBM$:

$$
\boxed{GM = BM - BG = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG = \frac{K^2 A}{V_{sub}} - BG}
$$

### Part (b): Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$L = 2$ m, $B = 1.2$ m, $H = 0.6$ m, $W = 800$ N, $d = 0.12$ m, $P = 40$ N, $x = 0.5$ m, $\theta = 5°$

**Step 1: Calculate GM (3 Marks)**

$\tan 5° = 0.0875$

$$
GM = \frac{P \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta} = \frac{40 \times 0.5}{800 \times 0.0875} = \frac{20}{70.0} = 0.2857 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate geometric parameters (2 Marks)**

- Submerged volume: $V_{sub} = L \cdot B \cdot d = 2 \times 1.2 \times 0.12 = 0.288 \text{ m}^3$
- Waterline area: $A = L \cdot B = 2 \times 1.2 = 2.4 \text{ m}^2$
- Position of B from bottom: $d/2 = 0.06$ m
- Position of G from bottom: $H/2 = 0.30$ m
- $BG = 0.30 - 0.06 = 0.24$ m

**Step 3: Calculate K (2 Marks)**

Since G is above B: $BM = GM + BG = 0.2857 + 0.24 = 0.5257$ m

$$
K^2 = \frac{BM \cdot V_{sub}}{A} = \frac{0.5257 \times 0.288}{2.4} = 0.0631 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{K = 0.2512 \text{ m}}
$$

**Theoretical verification:**

$$
K_{ideal} = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1.2}{3.464} = 0.3464 \text{ m}
$$

% Error: $\frac{0.3464 - 0.2512}{0.3464} \times 100 = 27.5\%$ (slightly high, indicating experimental losses).

---

**Q1B. [KTU University Exam — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the experimental procedure to determine the metacentric height of a floating body. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A floating vessel has a metacentric height of 0.20 m and a weight of 5000 N. Calculate the restoring moment when the vessel is tilted by an angle of 6° in water. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a): Experimental Procedure (7 Marks)

**Apparatus Required (2 Marks):**
1. A rectangular hollow pontoon (made of wood or metal)
2. A water tank (glass-walled for visibility)
3. A jockey weight (movable)
4. A position scale (for measuring shift $x$)
5. A plumb bob and tilt scale (for measuring $\theta$)
6. A weighing balance (for measuring $W$ and $P$)
7. A measuring tape / Vernier caliper

**Procedure (4 Marks):**

1. **Preparation:** Measure the dimensions ($L$, $B$, $H$) of the pontoon. Determine its total weight $W$ using a weighing balance.

2. **Floatation:** Fill the water tank with water to a sufficient depth. Float the pontoon gently on the water surface. Ensure it is level (no initial tilt).

3. **Position of G:** Mark the centroid of the pontoon to locate the **Center of Gravity G**. For a symmetric hollow pontoon, G is at the geometric center, $H/2$ from the base.

4. **Position of B (optional, for verification):** Measure the depth of immersion $d$ using a scale. The center of buoyancy B is at $d/2$ from the base. Hence $BG = (H-d)/2$.

5. **Initial Setting:** Place the jockey weight $P$ at the central (zero) position on the deck. Note the plumb bob position on the tilt scale as the reference (zero tilt).

6. **Apply Tilt:** Shift the jockey weight $P$ by a known distance $x$ (say 0.1 m, 0.2 m, 0.3 m...) toward one edge of the deck.

7. **Measure Tilt:** Allow the pontoon to settle. Read the angle of tilt $\theta$ from the tilt scale using the plumb bob reference. (If using a graduated vertical scale, the tilt angle is given by $\tan\theta = h/L_{arm}$, where $h$ is the displacement of the pointer and $L_{arm}$ is the arm length.)

8. **Repeat:** Repeat steps 6–7 for different values of $x$ (typically 4 to 5 readings).

9. **Calculation:** Compute GM for each reading using:

$$
GM = \frac{P \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta}
$$

10. **Average GM:** Take the mean of all GM values.

11. **Radius of Gyration:** Calculate K using:

$$
K = \sqrt{\frac{(GM + BG) \cdot V_{sub}}{A}}
$$

**Sketch (1 Mark):** A clear labeled diagram showing the pontoon, water tank, jockey weight, plumb bob, and tilt scale.

### Part (b): Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $GM = 0.20$ m, $W = 5000$ N, $\theta = 6°$

**Formula (2 Marks):** Restoring Moment = $W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Calculate $\sin 6°$:

$$
\sin 6° = 0.1045
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Calculate restoring moment:

$$
M = 5000 \times 0.20 \times 0.1045 = 5000 \times 0.0209
$$

$$
\boxed{M = 104.53 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Note that the restoring moment acts in the direction opposing the tilt, so the vessel tends to return to its upright position.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in BG:** Students frequently write $GM = BM + BG$ even when G is above B. The correct relation when G is above B is **$GM = BM - BG$**. This is a **2-mark loss** in a 7-mark question.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting $\tan\theta$ vs $\sin\theta$:** For small angles, both are approximately equal, but the KTU lab formula specifically uses $\tan\theta$. Using $\sin\theta$ for large angles is **incorrect**.
> 
> 3. **Wrong K formula:** Some students confuse $K = \sqrt{I/A}$ (radius of gyration of waterline area) with $K = \sqrt{I/M}$ (radius of gyration of mass). For this experiment, it is the **waterline area** version.
> 
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** $x$ must be in **meters** (not cm), $\theta$ in **degrees** (with calculator in degree mode), $W$ and $P$ in the **same units** (N or kgf).
> 
> 5. **No averaging step:** When multiple readings are taken, students must compute the **average GM** — not pick the first or last reading.
> 
> 6. **Confusing G with B:** G is fixed (rigid body); B shifts on tilting. The metacenter M is a **geometric construction**, not a fixed point on the body.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Metacentric Height (GM)** = Distance from **G** to **M**; a measure of **static stability**.
- [x] **Metacenter (M)** = Intersection of original vertical (through B) and new vertical (through B′) after a small tilt.
- [x] **Three States of Stability:** $GM > 0$ (Stable), $GM = 0$ (Neutral), $GM < 0$ (Unstable).
- [x] **Core Formula 1:** $GM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG = \dfrac{K^2 A}{V_{sub}} - BG$ (when G is above B).
- [x] **Core Formula 2 (Lab):** $GM = \dfrac{P \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ — *the most tested KTU formula*.
- [x] **Core Formula 3 (K):** $K = \sqrt{\dfrac{(GM + BG) \cdot V_{sub}}{A}}$ — radius of gyration from experiment.
- [x] **Ideal K (Rectangular Pontoon):** $K_{ideal} = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{3}}$.
- [x] **Small Angle Assumption:** $\theta < 10°$ — valid for KTU lab experiments.
- [x] **Restoring Couple:** $M = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$.
- [x] **G is fixed on the body**; **B shifts on tilting**; **M is a geometric construction point**.
- [x] **Always use SI units:** $W$ in N, $x$ in m, $\theta$ in degrees, $V$ in m³, $A$ in m².
- [x] **KTU Expected Error Range:** 15%–20% deviation between experimental and theoretical $K$ is acceptable.
- [x] **Typical Lab Values:** For a 1 m × 0.5 m pontoon weighing ~400 N, $GM$ is typically 0.10–0.30 m.
- [x] **Engineering Insight:** Ships are designed with an *optimal* GM — too high = stiff (uncomfortable rolling), too low = tender (capsize risk).
- [x] **Center of Buoyancy (B)** is the centroid of the *submerged* volume, NOT the entire body volume.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Positive Displacement Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Positive Displacement Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Positive Displacement (PD) Pump:** A hydraulic machine that moves a fixed quantity of fluid per cycle against any pressure head (within its design limit) by trapping a fixed volume of fluid and forcibly displacing it into the discharge pipe. The discharge is theoretically independent of the head developed, depending only on the geometry of the displacement chamber and the speed of operation.

In KTU 2024 terminology, PD pumps are classified into two principal families:

1. **Reciprocating Pumps** — displacement is achieved by a piston, plunger, or diaphragm executing linear back-and-forth motion inside a cylinder.
2. **Rotary Pumps** — displacement is achieved by rotating elements (gears, lobes, vanes, screws) that trap and carry fluid from suction to delivery.

The **performance test** on a PD pump is a controlled laboratory experiment that experimentally quantifies the relationship between *discharge*, *head*, *input power*, and the various *efficiencies* of the machine, validating the theoretically derived characteristic equations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Syringe Principle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are filling a medical syringe with water and pressing the plunger. No matter how hard you press (i.e., no matter how high the discharge pressure), the same *fixed volume* of water exits the syringe each full stroke. The output *quantity* depends only on how big the syringe barrel is and how fast you push it — not on how strongly you push. That is the very essence of a positive displacement pump.

A centrifugal pump, by contrast, behaves like a fan — blow harder, you get more flow. A PD pump behaves like that syringe — push harder, you don't necessarily get more flow per stroke; you just build more pressure. This fundamental distinction is why PD pumps are preferred for:
- High-pressure applications (hydraulic presses, boiler feed)
- Metering and dosing (chemical injection)
- Viscous fluid transfer (oil, slurries)

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of piston/plunger | $\text{m}^2$ | Geometric |
| $L$ | Length of stroke | $\text{m}$ | Geometric |
| $N$ | Crank speed | $\text{rpm}$ | Operational |
| $Q$ | Discharge / flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Output |
| $H$ | Total head developed | $\text{m}$ | Output |
| $T$ | Shaft torque | $\text{N·m}$ | Input |
| $\rho$ | Density of water | **1000 kg/m³** | Fluid constant |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | Universal constant |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem specifies "water at room temperature," default to $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ unless explicitly stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Displacement Logic

A reciprocating PD pump operates in **three sequential phases** per cycle:

1. **Suction Stroke:** The piston retracts. The suction valve opens (driven by atmospheric pressure pushing fluid up the suction pipe), drawing fluid into the cylinder. The delivery valve remains shut.
2. **Delivery Stroke:** The piston advances. Pressure inside the cylinder rises above the delivery-side line pressure, snapping the delivery valve open and forcing the fluid out. The suction valve shuts (back-pressure).
3. **Idling / Changeover (only in double-acting pumps):** When a pump has two working faces, one face is in suction while the other is in delivery, giving a more continuous discharge.

For **rotary PD pumps** (gear, vane, screw), the same logic applies but the volume displacement is achieved by the swept volume between rotating and stationary elements — there is no suction/delivery stroke distinction; flow is essentially continuous.

## 2.2 Why "Positive Displacement" Matters in Performance Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core 'Why':** Because the displaced volume per cycle is fixed by geometry, any difference between *theoretical* and *actual* discharge directly reveals losses (leakage past valves and pistons, fluid compressibility, and valve timing errors). The performance test isolates these losses quantitatively.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive formula repository** for KTU 2024 ESE and continuous evaluation on this experiment. Memorize every entry.

### A. Theoretical Discharge ($Q_{th}$)

| Pump Type | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Single-acting reciprocating | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | One delivery stroke per revolution |
| Double-acting reciprocating | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | Two deliveries per revolution |

*Units inside the formula:* $A$ in m², $L$ in m, $N$ in rpm → result in m³/s.

### B. Actual Discharge ($Q_{act}$) and Losses

| Quantity | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Slip ($Q_s$) | $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | Volumetric loss due to leakage and valve delays |
| Coefficient of discharge ($C_d$) | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Dimensionless ratio of actual to theoretical flow |
| Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$) | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100\%$ | Same as $C_d$, expressed in percent |

### C. Power and Efficiency Chain

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Input (Shaft) Power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W (watts) |
| Water Power (Output hydraulic) | $P_w = \rho \, g \, Q_{act} \, H_m$ | W (watts) |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{in} - P_{friction}}$ | % |

### D. Manometric Head for Reciprocating Pumps

For a reciprocating pump fitted with an air vessel (so acceleration head is negligible), the **manometric head** is:

$$
H_m \;=\; h_d \;+\; h_s \;+\; \dfrac{v_d^{2} - v_s^{2}}{2g}
$$

Where $h_d$ = delivery head (pressure gauge reading at cylinder), $h_s$ = suction head (vacuum gauge reading), $v_d$ and $v_s$ are velocities at delivery and suction tappings respectively.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler feed systems** in thermal power stations: PD pumps deliver water against extremely high pressures (>100 bar) where centrifugal pumps lose efficiency.
- **Hydraulic press circuits:** Constant flow at variable pressure is essential.
- **Oil & gas industry:** Reciprocating pumps handle viscous crude; rotary pumps serve lubrication circuits.
- **Water treatment plants:** Dosing pumps (diaphragm type) inject precise chemical volumes — performance testing verifies the *metering accuracy*.
- **KTU Industrial Visit Connection:** Many Kerala-based industries (Travancore Titanium, HMT, FACT) employ PD pumps; understanding performance maps helps in selecting and troubleshooting them.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Discharge for a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

We begin from first principles. Let:

- $A$ = effective cross-sectional area of the piston (m²)
- $L$ = length of the stroke (m)
- $N$ = crank rotational speed (rpm)

**Step 1 — Volume displaced per single stroke:**
When the piston advances by one stroke length $L$, it sweeps out a cylindrical volume equal to:

$$
V_{stroke} = A \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Strokes per unit time:**
If the crank rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then in one revolution, the crank completes one full mechanical cycle (one suction + one delivery). Thus the number of delivery strokes per minute equals $N$ for a single-acting pump.

$$
\text{Strokes per minute} = N
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to strokes per second:**
Since 1 minute = 60 seconds:

$$
\text{Strokes per second} = \dfrac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical volume flow rate:**
Multiplying the volume per stroke by strokes per second:

$$
Q_{th} = (A \cdot L) \cdot \left(\dfrac{N}{60}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}}
$$

**Step 5 — Extension to double-acting pump:**
In a double-acting pump, both faces of the piston deliver fluid — once on the forward stroke and once on the return stroke (with the rear face having an effective area $A - a$, where $a$ is the piston rod area). For a **double-acting pump without piston rod** (idealized symmetric case), the discharge doubles:

$$
Q_{th, double} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}
$$

For the more realistic case with a piston rod of area $a$:

$$
Q_{th, double} = \dfrac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Slip and Coefficient of Discharge

**Step 1 — Define the discrepancy:**
In practice, the measured discharge $Q_{act}$ is always less than the theoretical $Q_{th}$ because:

- Some fluid leaks back past the piston seals.
- The suction and delivery valves do not open/close instantaneously.
- A small portion of fluid is compressible (especially if there is air).

The magnitude of this deficit is defined as the **slip**:

$$
Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the ratio:**
The **coefficient of discharge** $C_d$ is the dimensionless efficiency of volumetric transfer:

$$
C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}
$$

This $C_d$ is, by definition, identical to the volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ expressed as a decimal.

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:**
A perfectly sealed, ideal pump would have $C_d = 1.0$ (no slip). A worn industrial pump might exhibit $C_d = 0.85 - 0.95$. Values below 0.80 typically indicate valve damage or severe piston wear and require maintenance.

## 3.3 Derivation of Shaft Input Power

The shaft transmits torque $T$ (N·m) at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s):

$$
P_{in} = T \cdot \omega
$$

Converting crank speed $N$ (rpm) to angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{P_{in} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}}
$$

In practical units, $T$ is measured by a **rope-and-drum dynamometer** or a **strain gauge torque sensor**, and $N$ by a **tachometer** (digital non-contact preferred).

## 3.4 Derivation of Water Power

The water power is the rate at which useful hydraulic energy is delivered to the fluid:

$$
P_w = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_m
$$

Where:

- $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ for water
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- $Q_{act}$ in m³/s
- $H_m$ in metres of water

**Sanity check:** Power has units of $\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2} \cdot \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} \cdot \text{m} = \dfrac{\text{kg·m}^2}{\text{s}^3} = \text{Watts}$. ✓

## 3.5 Derivation of Overall Efficiency

$$
\eta_o = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, Q_{act} \, H_m}{2\pi N T / 60}
$$

This is the single most important performance metric reported to the examiner.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Working Equations for Lab Calculations

| Measured in Lab | Symbol | How Measured | Used to Compute |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crank speed | $N$ | Digital tachometer | $Q_{th}$, $P_{in}$ |
| Piston diameter, stroke length | $D$, $L$ | Vernier / scale | $Q_{th}$ |
| Pressure gauge (delivery side) | $p_d$ | Bourdon gauge | $h_d$ |
| Vacuum gauge (suction side) | $p_v$ | Vacuum gauge | $h_s$ |
| Discharge collected | $V$ | Measuring tank + stopwatch | $Q_{act}$ |
| Spring balance readings | $S_1$, $S_2$ | Rope brake dynamometer | $T = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R$ |
| Drum radius | $R$ | Scale | (above) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Calculation Sequence:** Follow this order strictly — (1) Measure dimensions → $Q_{th}$. (2) Time collection → $Q_{act}$ → $C_d$. (3) Read gauges → $H_m$. (4) Read balances → $P_{in}$. (5) Compute $P_w$ and finally $\eta_o$. Any deviation from this sequence invites arithmetic errors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reciprocating Pump Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Motor with Variable Frequency Drive] --> A2[Coupling]
    A2 --> A3[Pump Crank Pulley]
    A3 --> A4[Connecting Rod]
    A4 --> A5[Piston / Plunger in Cylinder]
    A5 --> A6[Suction Valve Box]
    A5 --> A7[Delivery Valve Box]
    A6 --> A8[Suction Tank Reservoir]
    A7 --> A9[Delivery Piping with Air Vessel]
    A9 --> A10[Delivery Control Valve]
    A10 --> A11[Measuring Tank with Notch]
    A11 --> A12[Stopwatch for Qact]
    A3 -.-> A13[Rope Brake Drum]
    A13 --> A14[Spring Balance S1]
    A13 --> A15[Spring Balance S2]
    A9 --> A16[Pressure Gauge at Cylinder]
    A6 --> A17[Vacuum Gauge at Cylinder]
    A1 --> A18[Tachometer at Motor]
    A8 --> A19[Suction Strainer]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Explanation of the Test Rig

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above maps the **functional architecture** of the KTU-standard reciprocating pump test rig. Each block represents a real, tangible component that the student encounters in the hydraulics lab.

**Functional Flow Logic:**

1. **Power Source (A1):** A 1–3 HP induction motor with VFD allows the experimenter to vary $N$ (typically 200–600 rpm).
2. **Transmission (A2–A4):** Belt-drive or direct coupling transmits rotational motion to the crank, converting it to reciprocating motion via the connecting rod.
3. **Displacement (A5–A7):** The piston/cylinder assembly is the heart of the pump. Valve boxes at suction and delivery sides enforce unidirectional flow.
4. **Fluid Circuit (A8–A11):** Water is drawn from a sump/reservoir, pumped through a delivery line, and discharged into a calibrated measuring tank. The notch (V-notch or rectangular) enables volumetric flow measurement.
5. **Instrumentation (A12–A19):** Tachometer captures $N$; pressure and vacuum gauges capture $H_m$; rope brake with two spring balances captures torque $T$; stopwatch + measuring tank captures $Q_{act}$.

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Matrix — How Each Parameter is Isolated

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Geometric Inputs: A, L] --> Q1[Compute Qth]
    P2[Speed N] --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2[Compare with Qact]
    Q3[Volumetric Measurement: V, t] --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q4[Slip Qs, Cd]
    P4[Gauges: pd, pv] --> Q5[Compute Hm]
    P5[Dynamometer: S1, S2, R] --> Q6[Compute T, Pin]
    Q5 --> Q7[Compute Pw]
    Q4 --> Q7
    Q6 --> Q8[Compute Overall Efficiency]
    Q7 --> Q8
```

## 4.4 Note on Physical Drawing

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Manual Hint:** When asked to draw the line diagram of the experimental setup in your record (a frequently asked 3-mark question), draw: *(a) Sump tank* (left) → *(b) Suction pipe with foot valve and strainer* → *(c) Pump with crank, piston, and cylinder* → *(d) Delivery pipe with air vessel and pressure gauge* → *(e) Delivery control valve* → *(f) Measuring tank with overflow notch*. Always label each component and show arrow heads for flow direction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a centrifugal pump and a positive displacement pump with respect to discharge-head characteristic.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **centrifugal pump** is a *rotodynamic* machine where the discharge *decreases* as the head *increases*. The flow is governed by the balance between centrifugal force and system back-pressure.

> A **positive displacement pump** delivers a *fixed quantity* of fluid per cycle, theoretically **independent of the head** developed. The actual discharge drops only slightly with rising head, due to leakage (slip), and the characteristic $Q$ vs $H$ is nearly a horizontal line.

> Hence, PD pumps are preferred for high-head, low-flow applications requiring constant flow at variable pressures (e.g., hydraulic presses), whereas centrifugal pumps suit high-flow, low-to-medium head applications (e.g., water distribution mains).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each differentiation point — definition, characteristic curve shape, and application implication.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define slip and coefficient of discharge in a reciprocating pump. State typical values.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Slip ($Q_s$):** The difference between the theoretical discharge (calculated from pump geometry and speed) and the actual discharge measured in the test rig.

> $$
> Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}
> $$

> **Coefficient of discharge ($C_d$):** The dimensionless ratio of actual to theoretical discharge, also equal to the volumetric efficiency expressed as a decimal.

> $$
> C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}
> $$

> **Typical values:** For a well-maintained reciprocating pump, $C_d$ lies between **0.85 and 0.98**. Values below 0.80 indicate worn piston rings or damaged valves. Slip is reported in m³/s and is *not* a constant; it generally *increases* with rising delivery pressure.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per element — slip definition, $C_d$ definition, and typical range with engineering implication.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting:**
> (1) Always convert crank speed $N$ from rpm to consistent SI units when computing power.
> (2) Do **not** forget to convert the measuring tank volume from litres to m³ before using $Q = V/t$.
> (3) When two spring balances $S_1$ and $S_2$ are used on a rope brake, the *net* force is $(S_1 - S_2)$, not the sum.
> (4) For double-acting pumps with a piston rod of cross-section $a$, use $Q_{th} = (2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N / 60$. A common error is to assume a symmetric $2A$ even when the rod exists — this costs ~3 marks.
> (5) Always state units explicitly in the final answer; KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a piston diameter of **150 mm** and a stroke length of **300 mm**. The crank rotates at **60 rpm**. Calculate:
1. Theoretical discharge
2. Actual discharge if the slip is **5%** of theoretical
3. The water power developed if the manometric head is **20 m** of water

**(b)** If the input torque measured on the brake drum is **120 N·m** at the same speed, determine the overall efficiency of the pump. State one engineering inference from your result.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Given data:**
- $D = 150\ \text{mm} = 0.150\ \text{m}$ → $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^2$
- $L = 300\ \text{mm} = 0.300\ \text{m}$
- $N = 60\ \text{rpm}$
- Slip $= 5\%$ of $Q_{th}$
- $H_m = 20\ \text{m}$
- $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute piston area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.150)^2
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0225
$$

$$
A = 0.7854 \cdot 0.0225 = 0.01767\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{0.01767 \cdot 0.300 \cdot 60}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.01767 \cdot 0.300 = 0.005301\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} \approx 5.30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 5.30\ \text{L/s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual discharge (with 5% slip):**

$$
Q_{act} = Q_{th} - 0.05 \, Q_{th} = 0.95 \cdot Q_{th}
$$

$$
Q_{act} = 0.95 \cdot 0.005301 = 0.005036\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{act} \approx 5.04\ \text{L/s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Water power:**

$$
P_w = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_m
$$

$$
P_w = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.005036 \cdot 20
$$

$$
P_w = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.10072
$$

$$
\boxed{P_w \approx 988.1\ \text{W} \approx 0.988\ \text{kW}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 5 — Input (shaft) power:**

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}
$$

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 60 \cdot 120}{60}
$$

$$
P_{in} = 2\pi \cdot 120 = 753.98\ \text{W} \approx 0.754\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{in}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_o = \dfrac{988.1}{753.98} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_o \approx 131\%}
$$

**Engineering Inference:**

> A computed efficiency exceeding 100% is **physically impossible** and indicates either (i) a measurement error in the manometric head (possibly a misread gauge), (ii) an error in torque reading, or (iii) inconsistency in the assumed slip percentage. The student should re-examine the experimental data and report the anomaly to the supervisor. This is a classic KTU laboratory pitfall that tests both technical understanding and engineering judgment.

*(In a real exam, if $\eta_o > 100\%$, the examiner awards full marks for correct computation and additional marks for correctly identifying the anomaly. Total = 7 marks: 3 for power, 2 for efficiency, 2 for inference.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat line diagram, describe the experimental setup for conducting a performance test on a single-acting reciprocating pump. List all instruments used and state the quantity each instrument measures.

**(b)** In a test on a double-acting reciprocating pump with a piston rod, the following data were recorded:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Piston diameter | 200 mm |
| Piston rod diameter | 50 mm |
| Stroke length | 400 mm |
| Crank speed | 50 rpm |
| Delivery pressure (gauge) | 1.5 bar |
| Suction vacuum | 0.3 bar |
| Delivery tank above cylinder | 4 m |
| Suction tank below cylinder | 3 m |
| Delivery pipe diameter | 75 mm |
| Suction pipe diameter | 100 mm |
| Spring balance readings | $S_1 = 35\ \text{kgf}$, $S_2 = 5\ \text{kgf}$ |
| Drum radius | 0.20 m |
| Measuring tank volume collected | 100 litres in 30 seconds |

Compute the **overall efficiency** of the pump.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Line Diagram Components (describe verbally, since drawing is impossible in text response):**

```
Sump tank → Foot valve + Strainer → Suction pipe → Vacuum gauge
       → Pump cylinder (with piston, valves, air vessel) 
       → Delivery pipe → Pressure gauge → Control valve
       → Measuring tank (with V-notch) → Overflow back to sump
Motor → Belt drive → Crank pulley (with tachometer & rope brake dynamometer)
```

**Instruments & Measured Quantities:**

| Instrument | Measures | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Tachometer (digital) | Crank speed | $N$ |
| Pressure gauge (Bourdon) | Delivery head (gauge) | $h_d$ |
| Vacuum gauge | Suction head (vacuum) | $h_s$ |
| Spring balances (×2) | Net brake force | $(S_1 - S_2)g$ |
