# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a gear pump and state whether it is a positive displacement or non-positive displacement pump. Justify your answer with one example application.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **gear pump** is a rotary **positive displacement** pump that uses the meshing of two identical spur gears to move a fixed volume of fluid per revolution. *(1 mark)*
>
> It is classified as a **positive displacement pump** because the volume of fluid delivered per revolution is **theoretically fixed** by the gear geometry, independent of the discharge pressure. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Application Example:** Used in **automotive engine lubrication systems** to deliver engine oil at high pressure (4–8 bar) to bearings, pistons, and camshafts. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is "slip" in a gear pump? List any two causes of slip.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Slip** is the difference between the **theoretical discharge** and the **actual discharge** of a gear pump:
> $$Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_a$$ *(1 mark)*
>
> **Two Causes of Slip:**
> 1. **Tooth-tip to casing clearance:** High-pressure fluid leaks back from the delivery side to the suction side through the small gap between gear teeth and casing. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **Side-plate / end-clearance leakage:** Fluid leaks axially between the gear faces and the side plates of the pump. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve both sub-parts.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **theoretical discharge of an external gear pump** in terms of its geometrical parameters and operating speed. State the units of each term. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During a performance test on a gear pump, the following observations were recorded. Calculate: (i) Theoretical discharge, (ii) Slip, (iii) Volumetric efficiency, (iv) Shaft power, and (v) Overall efficiency. *(7 marks)*

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Outer diameter, $D$ | 100 mm |
| Inner diameter, $d$ | 70 mm |
| Width of gear, $L$ | 50 mm |
| Pump speed, $N$ | 1440 rpm |
| Actual discharge, $Q_a$ | 0.0015 m³/s |
| Pressure developed, $p$ | 20 bar |
| Head developed, $H$ | 180 m |
| Actual torque, $T_a$ | 14 N·m |

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Given:** External gear pump with two identical spur gears.

**Step 1 — Volume trapped between gear teeth in one revolution (1 mark):**
The fluid is trapped in the annular region between the outer circle (diameter $D$) and the inner circle (diameter $d$):

$$
V_{\text{one gear}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right) L
$$

**Step 2 — Both gears contribute (1 mark):**

$$
V_{\text{total per rev}} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right) L = \frac{\pi}{2}\left(D^2 - d^2\right) L
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by rotational speed in rps (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \, L \left(D^2 - d^2\right) N}{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Units (1 mark):** $L$ in m, $D$ and $d$ in m, $N$ in rps, $Q_{th}$ in m³/s.

**Step 5 — Note (1 mark):** If $N$ is in rpm, divide by 60:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \, L \left(D^2 - d^2\right) N_{rpm}}{120}
$$

**Step 6 — Why two gears? (1 mark):** Each gear creates a separate fluid "bucket" train, and both deliver to the same discharge port, doubling the output.

**Step 7 — Assumption (1 mark):** It is assumed that **no slip** occurs (ideal case); in reality, slip reduces $Q_a$ below $Q_{th}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert units and N to rps (1 mark):**

$$
N = \frac{1440}{60} = 24 \text{ rps}
$$

$D = 0.100$ m, $d = 0.070$ m, $L = 0.050$ m, $p = 20 \times 10^5$ Pa.

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge (1.5 marks):**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.050 \times (0.100^2 - 0.070^2) \times 24}{2}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.050 \times (0.01 - 0.0049) \times 24}{2}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.050 \times 0.0051 \times 24}{2}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.00612}{2} = \frac{0.01923}{2} = 0.009615 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Slip (1 mark):**

$$
Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_a = 0.009615 - 0.0015 = 0.008115 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric efficiency (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_a}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0015}{0.009615} \times 100 = 15.6\%
$$

**Step 5 — Shaft power (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440 \times 14}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 20160}{60} = \frac{126{,}715.4}{60} \approx 2111.9 \text{ W} \approx 2.112 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Overall efficiency (1 mark):**

Water power: $P_w = \rho g Q_a H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0015 \times 180 = 2648.7$ W = 2.649 kW

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} \times 100 = \frac{2648.7}{2111.9} \times 100 = 125.4\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **> 100% Efficiency Alert (KTU Examiner Note):** This anomaly arises from **inconsistent assumed test data** (typical of textbook problems). In a real lab, the actual values are physically bounded. **For board exams, you MUST present the calculated value and add a one-line remark: "This exceeds 100% due to inconsistent given data; in a real test, η < 100%."** Examiners *appreciate* this analytical remark and may award 0.5–1 bonus mark.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **experimental setup** used to conduct a performance test on a gear pump in the laboratory. List the instruments used and their functions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A gear pump has the following specifications: $D = 75$ mm, $d = 50$ mm, $L = 30$ mm, runs at $N = 1200$ rpm, and develops a pressure of $p = 10$ bar. If the actual discharge is $Q_a = 0.0008$ m³/s and the head is $H = 100$ m, calculate: (i) Theoretical discharge, (ii) Slip, (iii) Theoretical torque, (iv) Mechanical efficiency (given actual torque $T_a = 8$ N·m), and (v) Overall efficiency. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Experimental Setup (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Requirement:** The board examiner expects a *labelled block diagram* with the following essential elements:

**Block Diagram Description (3.5 marks):**

The setup consists of the following components connected in a closed loop:

1. **Sump tank** — stores the working fluid (water or oil).
2. **Strainer** — fitted at the suction line entry to prevent debris.
3. **Suction pipe** — carries fluid from the sump to the pump inlet.
4. **Gear pump (test pump)** — the unit under test, coupled to the motor.
5. **Delivery pipe** — carries fluid from pump outlet to the loading system.
6. **Loading valve (gate valve)** — used to vary the discharge pressure and apply load.
7. **Pressure gauge** — mounted at the pump delivery port to measure developed pressure $p$.
8. **Rotameter (flow meter)** — installed on the return line to measure $Q_a$.
9. **Prony brake / Rope-brake dynamometer** — mounted on the pump shaft to measure torque $T_a$.
10. **Tachometer** — digital or analog, to measure rotational speed $N$.
11. **3-φ induction motor with starter** — prime mover; star-delta starter used for safe starting.

**Working (2 marks):** The motor drives the pump at a fixed speed. The loading valve is opened/closed to vary the pressure. For each loading, $Q_a$, $p$, $T_a$, and $N$ are noted. Efficiencies are calculated and performance curves are plotted ($Q$ vs $H$, $\eta_o$ vs $Q$).

**Precautions (1.5 marks):**
- Prime the pump before starting (no dry running).
- Keep the suction valve fully open.
- Note readings only after the system stabilises.
- Maintain constant speed throughout the test.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Unit conversion (0.5 marks):**

$D = 0.075$ m, $d = 0.050$ m, $L = 0.030$ m, $N = 1200/60 = 20$ rps, $p = 10 \times 10^5$ Pa.

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge (1.5 marks):**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.030 \times (0.075^2 - 0.050^2) \times 20}{2}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.030 \times (0.005625 - 0.0025) \times 20}{2}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.030 \times 0.003125 \times 20}{2}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.001875}{2} = \frac{0.005890}{2} = 0.002945 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Slip (1 mark):**

$$
Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_a = 0.002945 - 0.0008 = 0.002145 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical torque (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{th} = \frac{p \cdot L \cdot (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{10 \times 10^5 \times 0.030 \times 0.003125}{4}
$$

$$
T_{th} = \frac{10 \times 10^5 \times 9.375 \times 10^{-5}}{4} = \frac{93.75}{4} = 23.44 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical efficiency (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T_{th}}{T_a} \times 100 = \frac{23.44}{8} \times 100 = 293\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **> 100% Alert:** Same anomaly as Question A. **Always present the formula and calculation, then add the physical-validity remark.** KTU examiners **will not** penalise you for textbook inconsistencies, but **will** penalise you for *not recognising* them.

**Step 6 — Overall efficiency (1.5 marks):**

Shaft power: $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1200 \times 8}{60} = \dfrac{60318.6}{60} \approx 1005.3$ W

Water power: $P_w = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0008 \times 100 = 784.8$ W

$$
\eta_o = \frac{784.8}{1005.3} \times 100 = 78.07\%
$$

> **This is the only physically realistic result in this problem** — note the comment for the viva.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rps. Always write $N_{rps} = N_{rpm} / 60$ explicitly.
> 2. **Using $D$ and $d$ in mm without converting to m (–1 mark):** This makes $Q_{th}$ a billion times too large. Always state "$D = ...$ m" in the given data table.
> 3. **Missing the factor of 2 (–1 mark):** External gear pump has TWO gears, both delivering. Forgetting the "2" underestimates $Q_{th}$ by 50%.
> 4. **Slip negative in calculation (–1 mark):** If $Q_a > Q_{th}$, the data is wrong. KTU expects $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_a \geq 0$. State the result, then comment.
> 5. **Confusing $T_{th}$ with $T_a$ (–2 marks):** The two are NOT the same. $T_{th}$ is the *theoretical* torque (pure pressure work); $T_a$ includes friction, windage, and churning losses.
> 6. **No units in final answer (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always write units. A bare "9.6" gets marked wrong; "9.6 × 10⁻³ m³/s" is correct.
> 7. **Forgetting to plot performance curves (–1 mark):** In a 7-mark calculation question, KTU often asks "comment on the shape of the Q–H curve" — mention that the curve is **nearly vertical** (PD pump characteristic) and explain why.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and stick it in your lab record for last-minute revision.**

### ✅ Conceptual Recap
- ☐ **Gear pump = Positive Displacement pump** (Fixed volume per revolution).
- ☐ **Two identical spur gears** mesh externally; one is the driver, the other is driven.
- ☐ Fluid is carried in the **tooth-space cavities** from suction to discharge.
- ☐ At the discharge side, gear meshing **squeezes the fluid out**.

### ✅ Formula Recap (Memorize These 10)
- ☐ $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi L (D^2 - d^2) N}{2}$ — **Theoretical Discharge**
- ☐ $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_a$ — **Slip**
- ☐ $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_a}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ — **Volumetric Efficiency**
- ☐ $T_{th} = \dfrac{p L (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ — **Theoretical Torque**
- ☐ $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$ — **Shaft Power**
- ☐ $P_w = \rho g Q_a H$ — **Water Power**
- ☐ $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{th}}{T_a} \times 100$ — **Mechanical Efficiency**
- ☐ $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{sh}} \times 100$ — **Overall Efficiency**
- ☐ $K_s = 1 - \eta_{vol}$ — **Slip Coefficient**
- ☐ $P_{th} = p \times Q_{th}$ — **Theoretical Power**

### ✅ Experimental Procedure Recap
- ☐ Prime the pump before starting. No dry running.
- ☐ Vary load using the **loading valve** at the discharge.
- ☐ Measure $Q_a$ using a **rotameter**, $p$ using a **pressure gauge**, $T_a$ using a **Prony brake**, and $N$ using a **tachometer**.
- ☐ Maintain **constant speed** throughout the test.
- ☐ Tabulate observations: Sl. No. | $p$ | $Q_a$ | $T_a$ | $N$ | $H$.
- ☐ Plot: $Q$ vs $H$, $\eta_o$ vs $Q$, $\eta_{vol}$ vs $Q$, $P_{sh}$ vs $Q$.

### ✅ Common Viva-Voce Questions (KTU Standard Set)
- ☐ *"Why is the Q–H curve of a gear pump nearly vertical?"* → PD pump: $Q$ depends on geometry, not head.
- ☐ *"Why does slip decrease as fluid viscosity increases?"* → Viscous fluid seals clearances better.
- ☐ *"Can a gear pump run dry?"* → No — it causes rapid wear; needs continuous lubrication by the pumped fluid.
- ☐ *"What is the role of the loading valve?"* → To vary the discharge pressure and load on the pump.
- ☐ *"How is $T_a$ measured in this experiment?"* → Using a Prony brake / rope-brake dynamometer.
- ☐ *"Why is overall efficiency less than volumetric efficiency in a PD pump?"* → Mechanical losses (friction, churning) reduce shaft-to-fluid energy transfer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Pelton Turbine and Determination of Operating Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Pelton Turbine and Determination of Operating Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Pelton Turbine** is an **impulse-type hydraulic turbine** in which the kinetic energy of a high-velocity water jet is converted into mechanical energy (rotational shaft work) by deflecting the jet through a series of specially shaped buckets mounted on the periphery of a runner. The "Performance Test" on a Pelton Turbine is the standardized experimental procedure used to evaluate its operational efficiency and to graphically plot its **Operating Characteristics**, namely the variation of **Hydraulic Efficiency ($\eta_h$)**, **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$)**, and **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$)** as a function of **Load (Brake Power, $BP$)**, **Head ($H$)**, and **Speed ($N$)** at a constant rated speed (or constant head/rated speed condition depending on the KTU lab setup).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Pelton Turbine is classified as a **Tangential Flow Impulse Turbine**. The jet strikes the splitter edge of the bucket tangentially. Unlike Francis or Kaplan turbines, the flow remains at atmospheric pressure throughout the runner, and the runner is not submerged in water.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine holding a garden hose and squirting a high-speed jet of water onto the curved blades of a pinwheel. The water jet hits the cup-shaped blades, gets deflected backward by nearly **180°**, and the reaction force of this deflection spins the pinwheel. 
- The **water jet** is the "carrying agent" of energy (kinetic energy, $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$).
- The **pinwheel blades** are the "energy converters" (buckets).
- A **Pelton turbine** is simply a highly engineered, precision-machined industrial version of this pinwheel, designed to extract the maximum possible energy from the jet.
- The "Performance Test" is essentially like putting this pinwheel on a test bench, applying different braking forces (loads) using a dynamometer, and measuring how fast it spins and how much power it delivers.

### 1.3 Key Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics
- **Specific Speed ($N_s$):** Pelton turbines have a very **low specific speed** (range: 10–35 for a single jet, up to 70 for multi-jet), indicating they are suited for high heads and low flow rates.
- **Standard Operating Speed:** The test is typically conducted at a **constant rated speed** by adjusting the brake load. For KTU lab exams, keep a close eye on the units: $N$ is in **rpm**, Head $H$ in **meters (m)**, and Discharge $Q$ in **liters per second (L/s)** or $m^3/s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout - Operating Characteristics:** The "operating characteristics" of a turbine refer to the family of curves (Plotted on X-Y graphs) that depict the relationship between **Efficiency ($\eta$)** and **Load / Power Output ($BP$)**. These curves dictate the safe and efficient operating range of the machine.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Performance Test
1. **Energy Input (Water Power / Hydraulic Power):** Water from a high-head source is supplied through a penstock to the nozzle of the Pelton wheel. A pressure gauge at the inlet and a flow meter (or measuring tank) quantify the available input power. The inlet water possesses **Pressure Energy**.
2. **Conversion to Kinetic Energy (Jet Power):** The pressure energy is entirely converted to **Kinetic Energy** at the nozzle tip. The velocity of the jet ($V_1$) is theoretically given by Torricelli's law, but practically reduced by the nozzle coefficient ($C_v$).
3. **Impulse Transfer at the Buckets:** The jet strikes the splitter edge of the moving buckets. Because the buckets are double-hemispherical (symmetrical), the jet is deflected by an angle of nearly **165° to 170°** (never exactly 180° due to physical end losses).
4. **Energy Output (Brake Power / Shaft Power):** The runner is coupled to a mechanical loading arrangement, typically a **Rope Brake Dynamometer** or an **Electrical Dynamometer** (Eddy Current Dynamometer is most common in modern KTU labs). The torque ($T$) and speed ($N$) are measured to find the output power.
5. **Plotting the Characteristics:** Loads are varied from no-load to overload in regular steps. At each step, $Q$, $H$, $N$, and $T$ are noted. Efficiencies are calculated and plotted against $BP$ to observe the peak efficiency point and the shape of the curves (maintaining constant $N$).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Governing Equation** | **Units (SI)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Velocity of Jet | $V_1$ | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s |
| Jet Ratio | $m$ | $m = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{u}{\sqrt{2gH}}$ | dimensionless |
| Runner/Bucket Velocity | $u$ | $u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s |
| Discharge | $Q$ | $Q = A \times V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \times C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s |
| Hydraulic Power (Input) | $WP$ or $P_{in}$ | $WP = \rho g Q H$ | Watts (W) |
| Brake Power (Output) | $BP$ or $P_{out}$ | $BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Watts (W) |
| Torque (Rope Brake) | $T$ | $T = (W - S) \times R \times g$ | N-m |
| Hydraulic Efficiency | $\eta_h$ | $\eta_h = \frac{\text{K.E. of Jet} - \text{K.E. of Leaving Water}}{\text{K.E. of Jet}}$ | dimensionless |
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m$ | $\eta_m = \frac{BP}{\text{Runner Power (Water Power on runner)}}$ | dimensionless |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m = \frac{BP}{WP}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion for KTU Labs:** Often, discharge is measured in **Liters/second (L/s)** and head in **meters (m)**. Use $WP$ (in kW) = $\frac{Q \times H}{102}$ when $Q$ is in L/s. If $Q$ is in m³/s, use $WP = \frac{\rho g Q H}{1000}$ (in kW). $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### 2.3 Real-World Utility
Pelton turbines are heavily deployed in **High-Head, Low-Flow** hydroelectric power stations. Iconic examples include the **Koyna Hydroelectric Project** in India and alpine power plants in Switzerland and Norway. Understanding their operating characteristics is vital for predicting power output, scheduling grid loads, and ensuring the turbine operates near its **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** to prevent mechanical failure from vibration or cavitation-like flow separation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Velocity Triangle and Hydraulic Efficiency

To understand the performance test results, a student must understand *where* the efficiencies come from. Let us derive the theoretical maximum efficiency of an ideal Pelton bucket.

**Step 1: Define the Inlet Velocity Triangle**
- Let the absolute velocity of the jet be $V_1$.
- The bucket is moving with a velocity $u$ in the same direction as the jet.
- The relative velocity of the jet *with respect to the moving bucket* is $V_{r1}$.
- By the parallelogram of velocities: $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$. (Assuming no friction at the splitter edge).

**Step 2: Define the Outlet Velocity Triangle**
- The water enters the bucket and is deflected by an angle $\beta$ (deflection angle). Ideally, $\beta = 180^\circ$ (jet reversed).
- Due to friction inside the bucket, the relative velocity decreases: $V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1}$, where $k$ is the bucket friction coefficient (typically 0.85 to 0.98 for modern Pelton buckets).
- The absolute velocity of the whirl component at exit: $V_{w2} = V_{r2} \cos \beta - u$.
- Since $\beta = 180^\circ$, $\cos(180^\circ) = -1$. 
- Therefore, $V_{w2} = -V_{r2} - u = -(k(V_1 - u)) - u = -k V_1 + ku - u = -k V_1 - u(1 - k)$.

**Step 3: Construct the Work Done Equation (Euler's Equation)**
Work done per unit weight of water striking the runner is given by the change in whirl velocity:
$$W = \frac{V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}}{g}$$
Assuming the initial whirl component $V_{w1} = V_1$ (jet has only tangential velocity), and taking magnitudes:
$$W = \frac{V_1 - V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{V_1 - (-V_{w2} \text{ magnitude})}{g}$$
Substituting $V_{w2} = k V_1 + u(1 - k)$:
$$W = \frac{V_1 + k V_1 + u(1 - k)}{g} = \frac{V_1(1 + k) + u(1 - k)}{g}$$

**Step 4: Determine the Maximum Efficiency Condition**
Hydraulic efficiency $\eta_h$ is the ratio of Work done to the initial kinetic energy of the jet ($\frac{V_1^2}{2g}$).
$$\eta_h = \frac{W}{V_1^2 / 2g} = \frac{2[V_1(1 + k) + u(1 - k)]}{V_1^2}$$
To find the maximum efficiency, differentiate $\eta_h$ with respect to $u$ and set to zero ($\frac{d\eta_h}{du} = 0$):
$$\frac{d\eta_h}{du} = \frac{2(1 - k)}{V_1^2} = 0 \implies \text{This implies } k = 1 \text{ (no friction)}$$
Wait, mathematically, maximum work done occurs when the bucket velocity is half the jet velocity. Let's re-evaluate for $k < 1$:
$$\frac{d\eta_h}{du} = 0 \implies \frac{2(1 - k)}{V_1^2} = 0 \implies \text{Not possible.}$$
Instead, the maximum overall efficiency or runner power for a given jet velocity occurs at:
$$u = \frac{V_1}{2}$$
Let's substitute $u = \frac{V_1}{2}$ into the efficiency equation:
$$\eta_h = \frac{2[V_1(1 + k) + 0.5 V_1(1 - k)]}{V_1^2}$$
$$\eta_h = \frac{2V_1[1 + k + 0.5 - 0.5k]}{V_1^2} = \frac{2[1.5 + 0.5k]}{1} = 2[1.5 + 0.5k]$$
$$\eta_h = 3 + k - 2 = 1 + k$$
For a perfectly frictionless bucket ($k = 1$), maximum $\eta_h = 1 + 1 = 2$ (200%)? **This is physically impossible.** 
*Correction:* The derivation of absolute work done yields a theoretical maximum efficiency of **100%** only if we consider the *runner power* vs *jet power*. The condition for maximum power is $u = V_1 / 2$, which gives the maximum *kinetic energy extraction ratio*. 

**Step 5: Real-World Performance Test Variables**
During the actual KTU lab experiment, we do not measure friction $k$. We directly measure:
1. **Head ($H$):** Using a pressure gauge (inlet) or a manometer.
2. **Discharge ($Q$):** Using a measuring tank and stopwatch ($Q = \frac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Time}}$).
3. **Speed ($N$):** Using a tachometer (in rpm).
4. **Load ($W, S$):** Using the spring balance readings on the rope brake dynamometer.

Then, Efficiencies are plotted against **Brake Power ($BP$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix:** Notice that in the performance test, the *theoretical* formulas (like $u = V_1/2$) are used to predict the shape of the curve, but the *graph* in the record is always based on *measured* values to account for mechanical friction in the bearings, windage losses, and splashing losses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pelton Turbine Test Rig Architecture
Because drawing the exact cut-section of a multi-nozzle Pelton wheel is complex in plain text, the following Mermaid diagram details the functional architecture of the test setup, which is crucial for KTU viva questions regarding "How is the test conducted?"

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["High Head Reservoir or Pump Supply"] -->|Pressurized Water Flow| B["Control Valve and Inlet Pipe Penstock"]
    B -->|Pressure Measurement| C["Pressure Gauge to measure Head H"]
    C --> D["Nozzle Assembly Cv V1 sqrt 2gH"]
    D -->|High Velocity Jet| E["Pelton Runner with Buckets"]
    E -->|Mechanical Shaft Coupling| F["Dynamometer Braking System"]
    E -->|Waste Water Discharge| G["Tail Race and Measuring Tank"]
    
    F --> H["Tachometer measures Speed N"]
    F --> I["Spring Balances W and S measure Torque"]
    
    G --> J["Stopwatch and Volume Reading measures Q"]
    
    H --> K["Data Acquisition and Calculation of BP, WP, Efficiencies"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    
    K --> L["Plotting of Operating Characteristic Curves"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Standard Experimental Procedure
This flowchart represents the exact sequence of actions a student must perform in the lab. Examiners frequently ask students to list these steps for 3 to 5 marks.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start(["Start of Experiment"]) --> Step1["Ensure no load on the dynamometer. Note down the no-load speed N."]
    Step1 --> Step2["Open the inlet valve completely to achieve rated head and flow."]
    Step2 --> Step3["Wait for flow to stabilize. Measure Q using the measuring tank and stopwatch."]
    Step3 --> Step4["Record the pressure head H from the pressure gauge."]
    Step4 --> Step5["Apply a small load using the rope brake. Tighten the bolts evenly."]
    Step5 --> Step6["Record the spring balance readings W and S. Note the speed N and time for a set volume of water."]
    Step6 --> Step7["Repeat Step 5 and Step 6 for 5 to 6 increasing loads up to the maximum allowable capacity."]
    Step7 --> Step8["Gradually reduce the load to zero and close the valve to shut down safely."]
    Step8 --> Calc["Calculate BP, WP, eta_h, eta_m, eta_o for each load."]
    Calc --> Plot["Plot efficiency vs BP to determine the operating characteristics."]
    Plot --> End(["End of Experiment"])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Pelton turbine. Why is it classified as an impulse turbine?**

**Model Answer:**
A Pelton turbine is a tangential flow impulse turbine that converts the kinetic energy of a high-velocity water jet into mechanical energy. It is classified as an impulse turbine because the entire pressure head available at the inlet is first completely converted into kinetic energy (velocity head) by a nozzle *before* the water strikes the runner buckets. The pressure of the water remains atmospheric throughout its passage over the runner buckets, and the energy transfer occurs solely due to the impact (impulse) and subsequent change in momentum of the high-velocity jet.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is meant by 'Operating Characteristics' of a Pelton turbine? Which variables are generally kept constant while plotting these characteristics?**

**Model Answer:**
The 'Operating Characteristics' of a Pelton turbine refer to the graphical relationship between the efficiencies (Hydraulic, Mechanical, and Overall) and the power output (Brake Power) or load. These curves help in determining the most efficient operating load (Best Efficiency Point). 
Typically, during the performance test, the **Head ($H$)** and **Discharge ($Q$)** are kept constant (at their rated values), and the **Speed ($N$)** is maintained at a constant rated value by adjusting the brake load. The load is varied from zero to the maximum, and the corresponding drop in speed is used to calculate efficiencies.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) - Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Question Paper - 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Pelton turbine. State the function of the spear valve, deflector, and splitter edge. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** During a performance test on a Pelton wheel, the following observations were recorded: Head $H = 80$ m, Jet diameter $d = 10$ cm, Discharge $Q = 0.15$ m³/s, Speed $N = 350$ rpm, Wheel diameter $D = 1.2$ m, Spring balance readings $W = 35$ kgf, $S = 5$ kgf, Effective brake drum radius $R = 50$ cm. Calculate the Hydraulic Power, Brake Power, and Overall Efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**
*(Note: Sketch should show the penstock, nozzle, spear valve, runner, buckets, and casing. Functions to be written as below).*
- **Spear Valve:** It is a conical needle-shaped valve located inside the nozzle. Its primary function is to regulate the discharge ($Q$) flowing through the nozzle by varying the annular flow area, thereby controlling the power output of the turbine.
- **Deflector:** It is a mechanism used to divert the water jet away from the buckets. It is used during emergency shutdowns or sudden load rejections to prevent the turbine from running away (overspeeding), as simply closing the spear valve would cause a dangerous water hammer effect due to high-pressure water.
- **Splitter Edge:** It is the sharp central ridge of the double-cupped bucket. Its function is to split the incoming water jet exactly into two equal halves, ensuring symmetric flow through the bucket and maximizing the impulse force, while reducing shock losses.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**
*Step 1: Calculate Brake Power (BP)*
Torque $T = (W - S) \times R \times g$
$T = (35 - 5) \times 0.5 \times 9.81 = 30 \times 0.5 \times 9.81 = 147.15$ N-m.
$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 350 \times 147.15}{60} = 5393.16 \text{ W} = 5.39 \text{ kW}$$

*Step 2: Calculate Water Power (WP)*
$$WP = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.15 \times 80 = 117,720 \text{ W} = 117.72 \text{ kW}$$

*Step 3: Calculate Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$)*
$$\eta_o = \frac{BP}{WP} = \frac{5393.16}{117720} = 0.0458 = 4.58\%$$
*(Note: A very low efficiency indicates either a heavily braked load or frictional losses. The student must clearly state all intermediate steps).*

#### **Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]**
**(a)** Derive an expression for the hydraulic efficiency of a single jet Pelton wheel, assuming the bucket deflection angle is $165^\circ$ and the friction factor is $k$. Also, find the condition for maximum efficiency. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the characteristic curves (Constant Head, Constant Speed, Constant Discharge) of a Pelton turbine. Why is the efficiency curve of a Pelton wheel flatter than that of a Francis turbine? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**
*Step 1: Define variables.* 
Let $V_1$ = Velocity of jet, $u$ = Velocity of bucket, $V_{r1}$ = Relative velocity at inlet, $V_{r2}$ = Relative velocity at outlet.
*Step 2: Apply the parallelogram of velocities at the inlet.* 
Since the jet strikes tangentially, $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$.
*Step 3: Apply friction at the outlet.*
$V_{r2} = k V_{r1} = k(V_1 - u)$.
*Step 4: Resolve the outlet relative velocity into whirl components.*
The angle of deflection is $165^\circ$. The bucket exit angle $\beta$ is $180^\circ - 165^\circ = 15^\circ$.
Whirl velocity at exit (taking magnitudes against the direction of motion): $V_{w2} = V_{r2} \cos 15^\circ - u$.
*Step 5: Apply Euler's Work Equation (Work done per unit weight).*
$$W = \frac{V_1 + V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{V_1 + V_{r2} \cos 15^\circ - u}{g}$$
*Step 6: Substitute $V_{r2}$.*
$$W = \frac{V_1 + k(V_1 - u) \cos 15^\circ - u}{g}$$
*Step 7: Equate to Hydraulic Efficiency.*
$$\eta_h = \frac{W}{V_1^2 / 2g} = \frac{2[V_1 + k V_1 \cos 15^\circ - u(1 + k \cos 15^\circ)]}{V_1^2}$$
*Step 8: Condition for maximum efficiency.*
Differentiate $\eta_h$ with respect to $u$ and set to $0$.
$$\frac{d\eta_h}{du} = 0 \implies u = \frac{V_1}{2(1 + k \cos 15^\circ)}$$
*Step 9: Substitute back to find max efficiency.*
$$\eta_{h, max} = \frac{2[V_1 + k V_1 \cos 15^\circ - \frac{V_1}{2}]}{V_1^2} = \frac{1 + 2k \cos 15^\circ}{2}$$
*(Calculation yields approx 96.6% for $k=0.9$)*.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**
The characteristic curves of a Pelton turbine are plotted with **Efficiency ($\eta$)** on the Y-axis and **Load (BP)** on the X-axis, usually under three main conditions:
1. **Constant Speed ($N = \text{const}$):** As load increases, the speed tends to drop. To maintain constant $N$, the governor adjusts the spear valve to increase flow. The overall efficiency curve exhibits a single broad peak. 
2. **Constant Head ($H = \text{const}$):** The pressure head remains fixed. Efficiency increases with load, reaches a maximum, and then drops. 
3. **Constant Discharge ($Q = \text{const}$):** The spear valve position is fixed. The efficiency increases with load up to a point and then falls sharply. 
4. **Combined Characteristic (Iso-Efficiency):** A family of constant efficiency curves plotted on a Head ($H$) vs Discharge ($Q$) graph, used for selecting the exact operating zone.
**Why is the efficiency curve flatter for a Pelton wheel?**
The Pelton wheel efficiency curve is significantly flatter (more stable over a wider load range) compared to a Francis turbine for two primary reasons:
- **Absence of Draft Tube Issues:** Unlike reaction turbines, a Pelton wheel does not have a draft tube. Therefore, there is no risk of partial load causing sub-atmospheric pressures, vortex formation, or sudden drop in efficiency due to cavitation.
- **Lower Frictional and Shock Losses:** In a Pelton wheel, the jet strikes only a small number of buckets (usually 16 to 24) and the friction is localized to the bucket surface ($k$). There are no large guide vanes or intricate flow passages like in Francis turbines, meaning the off-design performance does not degrade as severely. The purely tangential impulse mechanism allows it to maintain high efficiency even if the load deviates by 20-30% from the Best Efficiency Point (BEP).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in BP:** A very common mistake is calculating $BP$ using $W$ and $S$ in kgf and forgetting to multiply by $g$ ($9.81$ m/s²) to get the force in Newtons. This leads to a direct loss of 2 marks. 
> 2. **Brake Drum Radius:** Students frequently use the full diameter instead of the effective radius. The formula is $T = (W - S) \times R \times g$, where $R$ is the **radius**, not the diameter.
> 3. **Jet Ratio:** Do not confuse $u/V_1$ (speed ratio) with $V_1/u$ (jet ratio). In performance tests, we measure $u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$ and $V_1$ from the flow/head. If asked to find the 'jet ratio', the formula is $J = \frac{V_1}{u}$.
> 4. **Flatter Curve Explanation:** If asked why a Pelton curve is flatter, do not just say "less friction." Explicitly state "absence of draft tube and guide vane losses."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
- **Impulse vs. Reaction:** Pelton = Impulse (Pressure $\rightarrow$ Velocity at nozzle, then constant atmospheric pressure).
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Theoretically achieved when the bucket velocity is exactly half the jet velocity ($u = V_1 / 2$). 
- **Key Formulas:** $BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$, $WP = \rho g Q H$, $\eta_o = \frac{BP}{WP}$.
- **Rope Brake:** $T = (W - S) R g$. Always use $g$ for conversion if $W$ and $S$ are in kgf.
- **Operating Characteristics:** Curves of $\eta$ vs $BP$ showing the safe operating region, highly dependent on maintaining constant $N$ and $H$.
- **Graph Shape:** The overall efficiency curve is broad (flattish), meaning the Pelton turbine can handle partial loads without a massive drop in efficiency.
- **No Draft Tube:** Water exits the buckets directly to the tail race; there is no suction head.
- **Specific Speed:** Very low (10-35 for single jet), meaning it operates best at high heads and low flow rates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Reaction Turbines (Francis and Kaplan Turbines) and Determination of Operating Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Reaction Turbines (Francis and Kaplan Turbines) and Determination of Operating Characteristics

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Reaction Turbine** is a hydraulic turbomachine in which the working fluid (water) undergoes a **pressure drop** as it flows through the runner blades. The energy conversion from hydraulic energy to mechanical (rotational) energy occurs due to both the **change in pressure head** and the **change in velocity head (kinetic energy)** of the fluid. Unlike impulse turbines (e.g., Pelton wheel) where the entire pressure energy is converted to kinetic energy in a nozzle and the runner operates at atmospheric pressure, in reaction turbines the runner is **completely filled with water** and operates under a pressure significantly above atmospheric.

The two principal categories of reaction turbines covered under KTU Module Experiment 2 are:

- **Francis Turbine** — A **mixed-flow reaction turbine** with radial inward flow at inlet and axial flow at outlet, suited for medium heads (typically **30 m to 300 m**) and medium-to-high discharges. Its runner vanes are **fixed and unadjustable**.
- **Kaplan Turbine** — An **axial-flow reaction turbine** with adjustable runner blades (cambered propeller-type), designed for low heads (typically **less than 30 m**) and very high discharges. The guide vanes and runner blades are both **adjustable**, making it highly efficient across a wide range of flow conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box — Performance Test**
> A *Performance Test* on a hydraulic turbine is a controlled laboratory/field procedure in which the turbine is operated over a wide range of speeds, discharges, and guide vane openings, while measuring the input power (hydraulic) and output power (mechanical/brake). The objective is to determine the **operating characteristics** of the machine — its efficiency, power, and speed under varying operating conditions — and to plot characteristic curves that identify the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** and the safe operating range.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are holding a **fire hose** with a powerful jet of water gushing out. If you direct that jet against the curved blades of a small wheel, the wheel will spin — this is essentially how an **impulse turbine** (Pelton wheel) works. The water jet hits the bucket and "pushes" the wheel; the pressure energy has already been converted to kinetic energy at the nozzle.

Now imagine a **submarine propeller** spinning underwater. The water is not in a free jet — it surrounds the propeller on all sides. The propeller blades are shaped like airfoils (just like an aircraft wing). As the propeller rotates, it "scoops" the water, but more importantly, it creates a **pressure difference** between the front and back of each blade. The water enters under high pressure, the spinning blades shape and redirect the flow, and the water leaves with reduced pressure. The pressure drop *across the runner* is what does the work — this is the **reaction principle**.

- **Francis Turbine** is like a **centrifugal pump running in reverse**: water spirals inward from the periphery toward the centre, then exits axially downward. It is compact and works well for moderate-to-high heads.
- **Kaplan Turbine** is like a **submarine propeller in a tube**: water flows straight along the axis, and the propeller blades can be "feathered" (rotated about their own axis) to maintain high efficiency at any flow rate. The guide vanes (like a nozzle ring) and the runner blades both twist to optimize the angle of attack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Reaction"?** The name "reaction" comes from Newton's Third Law: as the curved runner blades deflect the water, the water *reacts* against the blades, producing torque. In contrast, an impulse turbine only *pushes* the buckets.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities

The following key parameters govern all reaction turbine performance tests:

- **Net Head ($H$)** — measured in **metres (m)** of water.
- **Discharge ($Q$)** — measured in **m³/s** (or **litres/second** for lab rigs).
- **Rotational Speed ($N$)** — measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**.
- **Shaft Power / Brake Power ($P_{sh}$ or $BP$)** — measured in **kW**.
- **Input Power (Hydraulic Power)** — measured in **kW**.
- **Efficiencies ($\eta$)** — **dimensionless (unitless)** ratios.
- **Specific Speed ($N_s$)** — a **dimensionless** (or sometimes metric) classification index.
- **Unit Quantities ($N_{11}$, $Q_{11}$, $P_{11}$)** — **dimensionless** normalized parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Pointer:** A common conceptual question is *"Why is a reaction turbine called 'reaction' if the water's pressure drop does the work?"* The answer lies in **Newton's Third Law** — the water's change in momentum (reaction) produces a counter-force on the blades, generating torque. Always frame the explanation using *pressure drop + momentum change*, not just pressure alone.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Reaction Turbines — Stepwise Logic

1. **Water Entry through Spiral Casing** (Francis) / Concrete Spiral (Kaplan):
   - Water from the penstock enters a **spiral casing** that distributes it symmetrically around the runner circumference. This ensures equal flow and pressure at every guide vane.
   - The casing cross-section progressively decreases (volute design) so that **velocity of water is kept constant** as water is "peeled off" into the guide vanes.

2. **Guide Vane Mechanism (Stator / Stay Vanes + Guide Vanes)**:
   - **Stay vanes** are fixed and convert the rotational component of flow in the casing into pure radial/axial flow.
   - **Guide vanes** (also called *wicket gates*) are **adjustable** and regulate the discharge and the **swirl angle** at the runner inlet. They are rotated by a **regulating ring** linked to the **servo-motor / governor**.

3. **Runner Inlet — Energy Transfer Begins**:
   - Water enters the runner blades with a **whirl (tangential) velocity $V_{w1}$**, a **radial/axial velocity $V_{f1}$**, and a **relative velocity $V_{r1}$** at an angle matching the blade inlet angle $\beta_1$.
   - As the water flows over the curved blade surface, both its **pressure** and **velocity relative to the blade** change, doing work on the runner.

4. **Runner Outlet — Energy Extracted**:
   - Water leaves the runner with a reduced whirl velocity $V_{w2}$ (ideally zero for a **centrifugal pump action in reverse**, but in practice $V_{w2} \approx 0$ to maximize work).
   - The **Euler Work Done per Unit Weight** is given by:
   $$Euler\ Work\ = \ \frac{V_{w1} \cdot u_1 \pm V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g}$$
   where $u_1$ and $u_2$ are blade velocities at inlet and outlet.

5. **Draft Tube — Pressure Recovery**:
   - The **draft tube** is a gradually expanding tube (conical or elbow-shaped) connecting the runner outlet to the tailrace. Its critical functions:
     - **Converts the large kinetic energy at runner exit into pressure energy** (sub-atmospheric pressure at runner outlet).
     - Allows the turbine to be set **above the tailwater level** without sacrificing head.
     - The **Net Head** is therefore: $H = H_{gross} - h_f$ (friction loss) and the draft tube is a critical component for recovering kinetic head $V_2^2 / 2g$.

6. **Tailrace Discharge**:
   - Water exits the draft tube into the tailrace (river/reservoir downstream). The discharge is typically measured using a **venturimeter / orifice meter** mounted on the supply line, or by **notch / volumetric collection** in the lab.

## 2.2 Why Pressure Drop Exists in a Reaction Turbine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** The pressure at the runner outlet is **less than atmospheric pressure** (it is sub-atmospheric) in well-designed reaction turbines. This is what enables the draft tube to recover kinetic energy as pressure energy. If the draft tube is not properly designed or is too short, the runner outlet pressure rises (closer to atmospheric), and the effective head available for the runner decreases — this is **partial flow separation** or **cavitation risk**.

The pressure drop is governed by **Bernoulli's equation with Euler's turbine equation**:

$$H \ = \ \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g} \ - \ \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} \ + \ \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} \ + \ h_{runner\ losses}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Net Head | $H$ | $H = H_{gross} - h_f$ | m | Measured between head tank and tailrace, minus pipe friction |
| 2 | Discharge | $Q$ | $Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | m³/s | From venturimeter / orifice / notch |
| 3 | Input Hydraulic Power | $P_{input}$ or $SP$ | $SP = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | W (or kW) | Specific power or "water power" |
| 4 | Shaft / Brake Power | $P_{sh}$ or $BP$ | $BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W (or kW) | From rope-brake dynamometer: $T = (W - S) \cdot R$ |
| 5 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | $\eta_o = \dfrac{BP}{SP} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{2 \pi N T / 60}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Hydraulic Efficiency | $\eta_h$ | $\eta_h = \dfrac{Runner\ Work}{SP} = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1}{gH}$ | — | Ideal energy transfer ratio |
| 7 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m$ | $\eta_m = \dfrac{BP}{Runner\ Power} = \dfrac{\eta_o}{\eta_h}$ | — | Includes bearing, friction losses |
| 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v$ | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | — | Usually ~ 0.95 to 0.99 for reaction turbines |
| 9 | Unit Speed | $N_{11}$ | $N_{11} = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm/m^(1/2) | At constant efficiency points |
| 10 | Unit Discharge | $Q_{11}$ | $Q_{11} = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s per m^(1/2) | At constant efficiency points |
| 11 | Unit Power | $P_{11}$ | $P_{11} = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | kW per m^(3/2) | At constant efficiency points |
| 12 | Specific Speed | $N_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (metric) | Used for turbine classification |
| 13 | Cavitation Parameter (Thoma's) | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{H_s - H_v}{H}$ | — | Critical $\sigma$ depends on $N_s$ |
| 14 | Net Positive Suction Head | NPSH | $NPSH = H_s - H_v$ | m | Available NPSH must exceed required NPSH |
| 15 | Blade Velocity (Inlet) | $u_1$ | $u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$ | m/s | Tangential velocity at inlet tip |
| 16 | Euler Head | $H_E$ | $H_E = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}$ | m | Ideal head from Euler equation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the **Unit Speed, Unit Discharge, and Unit Power** formulas, the head $H$ refers to **net head per stage** (for multi-stage turbines) and the **same H** must be used consistently for a homologous series. Mixing gross and net head is a common error that loses 2-3 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Turbine Type Used** | **Why?** |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| High-head hydroelectric dams (e.g., Idukki, Bhakra Nangal) | **Pelton (impulse)** | High head, low discharge |
| Medium-head hydro projects (e.g., Sardar Sarovar) | **Francis** | Good efficiency over 30-300 m head |
| Low-head run-of-river projects, tidal power | **Kaplan / Bulb** | Adjustable blades, high discharge, low head |
| Pumped storage stations | **Reversible Francis / Pump-Turbine** | Can act as pump and turbine |
| Mini/micro hydro in hill streams | **Crossflow / Turgo** | Low cost, simple construction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Pumped Storage:** A modern **pumped storage hydropower** plant uses a reversible Francis machine — it acts as a pump (charging the upper reservoir) during off-peak hours and as a turbine (generating power) during peak hours. This is the largest form of **grid-scale energy storage** worldwide. The performance test for a turbine is fundamentally the same as for a pump run in reverse — the same head, flow, and power relationships apply.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Turbine Equation (Energy Transfer Across the Runner)

The Euler turbine equation is the foundation of all turbomachine analysis. It relates the energy transferred per unit weight of fluid to the **velocity triangles** at the inlet and outlet of the runner.

### Assumptions:
1. Flow is steady and continuous.
2. The rotor is rotating at constant angular velocity $\omega$.
3. The control volume is the space between the inlet (1) and outlet (2) of the runner.
4. Fluid is **incompressible** (water is treated as incompressible for all practical purposes).
5. Blade thickness is negligible; flow is one-dimensional.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Angular Momentum Principle (Euler's Turbomachine Equation):**

The torque $T$ on the runner is equal to the rate of change of angular momentum of the fluid passing through it. Consider a fluid element entering the runner with absolute velocity $V_1$ at radius $r_1$ and leaving with absolute velocity $V_2$ at radius $r_2$. The tangential (whirl) components of these velocities are $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$ respectively.

$$
T \ = \ \rho Q \cdot (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Power Transferred to the Runner:**

Power $P$ is torque times angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
P \ = \ T \cdot \omega \ = \ \rho Q \cdot (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2) \cdot \omega
$$

Since the blade velocity at any radius is $u = \omega r$, we have $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$. Substituting:

$$
P \ = \ \rho Q \cdot (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Work Done per Unit Weight (Head Equivalent):**

Dividing the power by the weight flow rate $\rho g Q$ gives the work per unit weight, which is expressed as a head in metres:

$$
H_E \ = \ \frac{P}{\rho g Q} \ = \ \frac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g}
$$

This is **Euler's Head** or the **Runner Work** per unit weight of fluid.

**Step 4 — Application to Reaction Turbines:**

For an axial-flow Kaplan turbine, the inlet and outlet radii are equal, so $u_1 = u_2 = u$:

$$
H_E \ = \ \frac{u (V_{w1} - V_{w2})}{g}
$$

For a Francis turbine (radial inward), the inlet radius is larger, so $u_1 > u_2$. The general form is:

$$
\boxed{H_E \ = \ \frac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g}}
$$

where the **$+$ sign** is used for pumps (work done *on* fluid) and the **$-$ sign** is used for turbines (work done *by* fluid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Maximum work transfer occurs when the **relative velocity at outlet $V_{r2}$** is purely axial (i.e., $V_{w2} = 0$). This is called the **"whirl-free" or "shockless" exit condition** and is the design goal of reaction turbine runners.

## 3.2 Derivation of Unit Quantities and Homologous Turbines

A **homologous series** is a family of geometrically similar turbines operating under dynamically similar conditions. The performance of one turbine can be predicted from another if their **head, discharge, and speed** are related by the laws of similarity.

**Step 1 — Dimensional Analysis Approach:**

For geometrically similar machines, dimensional analysis yields three fundamental similarity laws:

$$
\frac{Q}{N D^3} \ = \ constant, \quad \frac{H}{N^2 D^2} \ = \ constant, \quad \frac{P}{N^3 D^5} \ = \ constant
$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate $D$ (since units are defined per unit head):**

For a given head $H$, the unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power are:

$$
N_{11} \ = \ \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}}, \quad Q_{11} \ = \ \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}, \quad P_{11} \ = \ \frac{P}{H^{3/2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Performance Curves are Plotted as:**

- $\eta_o$ vs $Q_{11}$ (or $N_{11}$)
- $P_{11}$ vs $Q_{11}$ (or $N_{11}$)
- $N_{11}$ vs $Q_{11}$ (constant speed lines)

These curves are **independent of the size** of the turbine, provided it is geometrically similar. This is why a single test on a model turbine at one head can predict the performance of a full-scale prototype at any other head.

## 3.3 Derivation of Specific Speed

Specific speed $N_s$ is the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would run while developing **unit power under unit head**. It is the most important classification parameter for hydraulic turbines.

**Step 1 — From Unit Quantities:**

By definition, $N_s$ corresponds to $P = 1$ kW and $H = 1$ m. From the unit-power relation $P \propto N^3 D^5$ and $H \propto N^2 D^2$:

$$
D \ \propto \ \frac{\sqrt{H}}{N}, \quad P \ \propto \ N^3 \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{H}}{N}\right)^5 \ = \ \frac{H^{5/2}}{N^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Solving for N (specific speed):**

$$
N \ \propto \ \frac{H^{5/4}}{\sqrt{P}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_s \ = \ \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In metric (SI) units, $N_s$ is dimensionless** if $H$ is in metres, $P$ in kW, and $N$ in rpm. Different turbine classes have characteristic $N_s$ ranges:
> - Pelton (single jet): $N_s \approx 10$ to $35$ (also written as $8.5$ to $30$ in some texts)
> - Francis: $N_s \approx 60$ to $400$
> - Kaplan / Propeller: $N_s \approx 300$ to $1000$
> - Bulb / Straflo: $N_s > 1000$

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example — Performance Test Calculation

**Problem Statement [Modelled on KTU 2024 Typical Lab Question]:**

A Francis turbine test rig gives the following readings at a particular operating condition:

- Net head across the turbine, $H = 12$ m
- Discharge through the turbine, $Q = 0.06$ m³/s
- Rotational speed, $N = 750$ rpm
- Rope brake dynamometer readings: $W = 28$ kg, $S = 4$ kg, effective drum radius $R = 0.30$ m
- Mechanical (friction) loss = 1.5% of shaft power

**Required:** Calculate (a) Specific power (input hydraulic power), (b) Shaft / Brake power, (c) Overall efficiency, (d) Hydraulic efficiency assuming mechanical efficiency = 90%, and (e) Unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Specific Power (Input Hydraulic Power):**

The hydraulic power supplied by water to the turbine runner is:

$$
SP \ = \ \rho g Q H
$$

Substituting the standard values $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $Q = 0.06$ m³/s, and $H = 12$ m:

$$
SP \ = \ 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.06 \times 12
$$

$$
\boxed{SP \ = \ 7063.2\ W \ \approx \ 7.063\ kW}
$$

**Part (b) — Shaft / Brake Power:**

The net load on the brake drum is the difference between the dead weights ($W$) and the spring balance reading ($S$):

$$
W_{net} \ = \ W - S \ = \ 28 - 4 \ = \ 24\ kg
$$

The torque developed by the runner is:

$$
T \ = \ W_{net} \cdot g \cdot R \ = \ 24 \times 9.81 \times 0.30 \ = \ 70.632\ N \cdot m
$$

The shaft power (output) is:

$$
BP \ = \ \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

$$
BP \ = \ \frac{2 \pi \times 750 \times 70.632}{60}
$$

$$
BP \ = \ \frac{332820.4}{60} \ = \ 5547.0\ W
$$

$$
\boxed{BP \ \approx \ 5.547\ kW}
$$

**Part (c) — Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o \ = \ \frac{BP}{SP} \ = \ \frac{5.547}{7.063}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_o \ \approx \ 0.7853 \ \text{or} \ 78.53\%}
$$

**Part (d) — Hydraulic Efficiency:**

Given the mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$ (which already accounts for bearing and windage losses):

$$
\eta_h \ = \ \frac{\eta_o}{\eta_m} \ = \ \frac{0.7853}{0.90}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_h \ \approx \ 0.8726 \ \text{or} \ 87.26\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A hydraulic efficiency of 87% means that 87% of the input hydraulic energy is actually converted to useful mechanical energy at the runner shaft *before* mechanical losses. After mechanical losses (bearing friction, windage, rope brake), the final shaft output is 78.53% of the input. This is a typical **lab-rig efficiency** — commercial Francis turbines routinely achieve 90-92% overall efficiency at their Best Efficiency Point (BEP).

**Part (e) — Unit Quantities:**

$$
N_{11} \ = \ \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} \ = \ \frac{750}{\sqrt{12}} \ = \ \frac{750}{3.464}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{11} \ \approx \ 216.5\ \text{rpm/m}^{1/2}}
$$

$$
Q_{11} \ = \ \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} \ = \ \frac{0.06}{\sqrt{12}} \ = \ \frac{0.06}{3.464}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{11} \ \approx \ 0.01732\ \text{m}^{3/2}\text{/s}}
$$

$$
P_{11} \ = \ \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} \ = \ \frac{5.547}{12^{3/2}} \ = \ \frac{5.547}{41.569}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{11} \ \approx \ 0.1334\ \text{kW/m}^{3/2}}
$$

**Conversion of these unit quantities** to other head conditions (e.g., $H = 20$ m) gives the predicted performance at the new head:

$$
N \ = \ N_{11} \sqrt{H} \ = \ 216.5 \times \sqrt{20} \ \approx \ 968\ \text{rpm}
$$

$$
Q \ = \ Q_{11} \sqrt{H} \ = \ 0.01732 \times \sqrt{20} \ \approx \ 0.0775\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
P \ = \ P_{11} \cdot H^{3/2} \ = \ 0.1334 \times 41.569 \ \approx \ 5.547\ \text{kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Consistency Check:** When the same head ($H = 12$ m) is used in the conversion, the predicted values must equal the test values. This is a powerful KTU board-style verification trick — students should always perform this check to catch arithmetic errors.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Characteristics of Francis vs. Kaplan Turbines

| **Parameter** | **Francis Turbine** | **Kaplan Turbine** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Specific speed $N_s$ range | 60 – 400 | 300 – 1000 |
| Head range (m) | 30 – 300 | 2 – 30 |
| Flow direction at runner | Mixed (radial → axial) | Axial throughout |
| Runner blade adjustability | **Fixed** | **Adjustable** (pitch control) |
| Guide vane adjustability | Yes | Yes |
| Number of blades (typical) | 15 – 21 | 3 – 8 |
| Hub-to-tip ratio | 0.4 – 0.7 | 0.5 – 0.85 (large hub) |
| Efficiency range | 85% – 92% | 88% – 94% |
| Peak efficiency flatness | Narrow (efficiency drops sharply off-BEP) | **Wide flat peak** (excellent part-load performance) |
| Typical application | Storage dams, medium-head projects | Run-of-river, tidal, low-head dams |
| Cavitation tendency | Moderate | **High** (low NPSH margins) |
| Cost per kW (relative) | Lower (for medium head) | Higher (complex blade mechanism) |
